items.claim: 'Twenty percent of miners in Appalachia with twenty-five or more years of tenure have black lung' (line 66)

'Continued Increase in Prevalence of Coal Workers' Pneumoconiosis in the United States, 1970-2017,' American Journal of Public Health 2018;108(9):1220-1222, https://ajph.aphapublications.org/doi/10.2105/AJPH.2018.304517; NIOSH/CDC update https://archive.cdc.gov/www_cdc_gov/niosh/updates/upd-07-20-18.html.

As written ('Appalachia' + 20%) it overstates the geographic scope. FIX: change 'in Appalachia' to 'in central Appalachia' (KY/VA/WV) to make the 20% figure correct, citing Blackley et al. 2018 AJPH.

items.claim: J.S. Darling & Son took the name in 1884 when Frank Darling joined his father's oyster business; at peak 160 shuckers handled 200,000 bushels/year; oyster-shell mountains rose more than four stories; the Darlings ran a menhaden factory at Factory Point, Fox Hill, rendering thousands of tons/year (line 18).
items.source: J.S. Darling & Son Historical Marker, Hampton History Museum (Historical Marker Database, hmdb.org marker #166500, https://www.hmdb.org/m.asp?m=166500); Dictionary of Virginia Biography, 'Frank Wilkinson Darling,' Library of Virginia (https://old.lva.virginia.gov/public/dvb/bio.asp?b=Darling_Frank_Wilkinson).
items.note: PARTIAL. SOURCED & matches book: 160 shuckers + 200,000 bushels/year confirmed verbatim by the Hampton History Museum marker; firm renamed 'J.S. Darling and Son' in 1884 when Frank joined confirmed (DVB), though DVB dates the start of the oyster business to 1882. DISAGREES/UNSOURCED: (a) 'oyster-shell mountains rose more than four stories' — not found in any public source; flag as author/local-history claim needing the Hampton History Museum or Library of Virginia archival source. (b) The MENHADEN factory: James S. Darling owned a menhaden fish-oil factory by 1879, but a storm destroyed it and pushed him into the oyster business — i.e. the menhaden operation PREDATED and was destroyed before the oyster firm's peak. The specific 'Factory Point, Fox Hill' location and 'thousands of tons/year' rendering figure are UNSOURCED in public records. Recommend the chapter either drop or attribute the four-stories and Factory-Point/tonnage details to a named local-history source, and clarify the menhaden factory was an early/destroyed venture, not concurrent with the peak oyster plant.

items.claim: James McMenamin 'invented and patented a canning process' for cooked Chesapeake crabmeat in the 1870s (line 18).
items.source: Hampton History Museum, McMenamin & Co. collection (hampton.pastperfectonline.com); 'Crabtown' Historical Marker, Historical Marker Database (https://www.hmdb.org/m.asp?m=166499); Site Selection Magazine, 'Food and Beverage Steeped in Hampton Roads.'
items.note: SOURCED & broadly matches book. James McMenamin perfected/patented vacuum-sealed canned cooked crabmeat in the 1870s, built a large Hampton plant in 1879 (~20,000 sq ft, ~350 workers), giving Hampton its 'Crab Town' nickname; McMenamin & Co. crabmeat went on Peary's 1909 polar expedition. The exact USPTO patent number was not located in this pass (secondary sources describe the patent but did not surface the number) — if the chapter wants to cite the patent directly rather than the canning achievement generally, pull the specific patent from USPTO/Google Patents to lock the number. Claim as written is safe.

items.claim: Oyster collapse magnitude: ~15 million bushels/year in the late 1880s falling to under 200,000 by the 1990s; a 99% / two-orders-of-magnitude decline (line 38).
items.source: Virginia Institute of Marine Science (VIMS), 'Life Among the Oysters: Background' (http://masweb.vims.edu/lifeamongoysters/background.html); University of Maryland Center for Environmental Science (UMCES), 'History of oysters' (https://www.umces.edu/oysters/history); Wilberg et al. / Chesapeake Bay Program oyster pages.
items.note: SOURCED & matches book at the order-of-magnitude level. ~15 million bushels at the late-1800s peak is the standard figure for the MARYLAND portion of the Bay alone (some sources put bay-wide peak at ~20 million bushels in the 1880s). The 'late 1880s' framing is correct for the peak; the under-200,000-bushels/990s endpoint and 99% / two-orders-of-magnitude framing are consistent with VIMS/UMCES. Minor precision flag: specify whether 15M is Maryland-only or bay-wide to avoid an apples-to-oranges denominator with the under-200,000 endpoint.

items.claim: Disease timeline: MSX appeared in the late 1950s and Dermo in the years following, as drivers of oyster collapse (line 40).
items.source: VIMS Shellfish Pathology / Molluscan Health Lab; Burreson & Ragone Calvo, molecular-diagnostics review (FAO x4946e); VIMS, 'Long-term study shows oysters developing disease resistance' (https://www.vims.edu/features/programs/oysters_disease_resistance.php).
items.note: PARTIALLY CONTRADICTED — sequence is imprecise. MSX (Haplosporidium nelsoni) was first described in 1957 (Delaware Bay), reached the lower Chesapeake in 1959, and killed ~95% of oysters in that region by 1962 — so 'late 1950s' for MSX is correct. BUT Dermo (Perkinsus marinus) did NOT primarily follow MSX in the late 1950s/early 1960s in the Chesapeake; Dermo's emergence as a major Chesapeake mortality driver came with the drought years of the mid-to-late 1980s (and it remained rare north of the Chesapeake until ~1990). Recommend rewording so Dermo's major Chesapeake impact is dated to the 1980s rather than 'the years following' MSX, to avoid implying both diseases peaked together in the late 1950s.

items.claim: Maryland's 5-year commercial striped bass moratorium beginning 1985; fishery declared restored by 1995 (line 75).
items.source: Atlantic States Marine Fisheries Commission (ASMFC), Atlantic Striped Bass FMP / Amendment 5 (1995 'fully recovered' declaration); Maryland DNR Commercial Striped Bass page (https://dnr.maryland.gov/fisheries/Pages/striped-bass.aspx); NOAA Fisheries, 'Checking in on Chesapeake Bay Striped Bass.'
items.note: SOURCED & matches book. Maryland imposed a total moratorium in 1985; it lasted 5 years and the fishery reopened in 1990; ASMFC declared the coastwide Atlantic striped bass stock 'fully recovered' in 1995 (Amendment 5). The book's '5-year moratorium beginning 1985' and 'declared restored by 1995' are both accurate, but note the two dates describe different events (the 5-year moratorium ran 1985–1990; the 1995 'restored'/'fully recovered' declaration came 5 years after reopening). Wording is defensible as written.

items.claim: 2024 Maryland young-of-year striped bass index of 2.0 fish per sample vs. long-term average of 11.0, sixth consecutive below-average year (line 77).
items.source: Maryland DNR, 'Results of Chesapeake Bay 2024 Young-of-Year Striped Bass Survey Show Little Change,' Oct 17 2024 (https://news.maryland.gov/dnr/2024/10/17/results-of-chesapeake-bay-2024-young-of-year-striped-bass-survey-show-little-change/); MD DNR Juvenile Striped Bass Survey page (https://dnr.maryland.gov/fisheries/pages/striped-bass/juvenile-index.aspx).

DATE-LOCK FLAG (today 2026-06-04): this is the 2024 survey result; the 2025 survey result will have been released (typically each October), so verify whether the chapter should be updated to the most recent index and whether 'sixth consecutive' has become 'seventh.' Cite the 2024 survey explicitly by year so the figure does not silently go stale.

items.claim: Virginia closed its commercial shad fishery in January 1994; runs never recovered; the science 'has not converged' on why (lines 91, 55).
items.source: Virginia Marine Resources Commission (VMRC) American shad moratorium effective January 1, 1994; VIMS American Shad Monitoring Program (https://www.vims.edu/research/units/programs/american_shad/); Virginia DWR shad-restoration page.
items.note: SOURCED & matches book. The VMRC moratorium on taking American shad in the Chesapeake and its tributaries took effect January 1, 1994 (both recreational and commercial). Non-recovery is supported: James River stocking ran ~25 years and was discontinued in 2017 with no rebound; the Rappahannock has shown some increase. 'Science has not converged on why' is a fair characterization of the mixed recovery. Claim is well supported.

items.claim: Blue crab 2025 winter dredge survey: Bay-wide population ~238 million, second-lowest since the survey began in 1990, sixth consecutive below-average year (line 99).

items.note: SOURCED & matches book: 2025 estimate 238 million (down from 317M in 2024), second-lowest on record since the 1990 survey began; juvenile recruitment was the third-lowest and marked the sixth consecutive below-average juvenile year. DATE-LOCK FLAG (today 2026-06-04): a 2026 winter dredge survey has since been released reporting an increase (~46% rise per National Fisherman / WBOC coverage). The '238 million / second-lowest / sixth consecutive' snapshot is now the PRIOR year — cite explicitly as the 2025 survey, and decide whether to update to 2026 figures, since 'sixth consecutive below-average' may no longer hold.

items.claim: Menhaden Bay-specific cap set by ASMFC in 2006 and reduced in 2017 to 51,000 metric tons (line 115).
items.source: Atlantic States Marine Fisheries Commission (ASMFC), Atlantic Menhaden FMP / 2017 reduction of the Chesapeake Bay reduction-fishery cap to 51,000 metric tons; Chesapeake Bay Foundation, 'A Timeline of Menhaden Conservation' (https://www.cbf.org/a-timeline-of-menhaden-conservation/).
items.note: SOURCED & matches book. ASMFC first set the Chesapeake Bay reduction-harvest cap in 2006 and lowered it to 51,000 metric tons in 2017. Note (not a correction to the book's stated facts): as of late 2025 ASMFC initiated a new addendum on the Bay cap and reduced the 2026 coastwide TAC by 20% — if the chapter implies 51,000 mt is the current/standing cap, add a date-lock so a 2026 change does not strand the figure.

items.claim: VIMS drafted a $3 million Bay-specific menhaden study proposal in 2023 that the Virginia General Assembly declined to fund (line 117).
items.source: VIMS, 'Atlantic Menhaden Research Planning,' Oct 1 2023 report to the General Assembly (https://rga.lis.virginia.gov/Published/2023/RD528/PDF); Virginia Mercury, 'State study on menhaden in Chesapeake Bay would cost $2.6 million,' Oct 25 2023; WAVY, 'Budget amendment to help tiny fish is tossed by Virginia lawmakers.'
items.note: FIGURE DISAGREES + timeline imprecise. The study cost was reported at ~$2.6 million (the methodology/plan; the multi-year acoustic-tagging proposal has also been cited at ~$2.8M) — NOT $3 million. Recommend correcting to '$2.6 million.' Timeline nuance: in 2023 the General Assembly did not simply 'decline to fund'; it directed VIMS to develop a study methodology (published Oct 2023), and SUBSEQUENT budget amendments (2024 session) to fund the actual study were stripped/defeated. Reword to '~$2.6 million study VIMS scoped at the legislature's direction in 2023, which lawmakers then declined to fund' to be precise.

NAME RECONCILIATION: the official petition record spells it 'Tanya O'Connor' — the CHAPTER spelling ('Tanya O'Connor') is correct and the CONSENT-TRACKER spelling ('Tonya O'Connor') is the error to fix. The 349-page count is NOT shown on the Town Hall summary page (not contradicted, just not surfaced); verify the page count against the filed PDF if it is load-bearing, otherwise it can be softened to 'a several-hundred-page petition.'

items.claim: Public-trust case-law reporter citations as printed: Commonwealth v. City of Newport News 158 Va. 521 (1932); Taylor v. Commonwealth 102 Va. 759 (1904); Bradford v. Nature Conservancy 224 Va. 181 (1982) (line 123).

Supreme Court Records (https://scholarlycommons.law.wlu.edu/va-supreme-court-records-vol158/26/); Taylor v. Commonwealth, 102 Va. 759 (1904), W&M Eminent Domain Case Finder + JSTOR; Bradford v. Nature Conservancy, 224 Va. 181 (1982), Justia (https://law.justia.com/cases/virginia/supreme-court/1982/791288-1.html).
items.note: ALL THREE REPORTER CITATIONS VERIFIED ACCURATE as printed. (1) Commonwealth v. City of Newport News confirmed at 158 Va. 521 (1932), parallel cite 164 S.E. 689. (2) Taylor v. Commonwealth confirmed at 102 Va. 759 (1904) — Virginia Supreme Court of Appeals, oyster/riverbed public-trust case (beds of rivers belong to the Commonwealth for the benefit of all citizens). items.claim: Tangier Island: losing ~15 feet of shoreline per year; continuously inhabited since the 1770s; two square miles (line 156).
items.source: U.S. Army Corps of Engineers 2015 Tangier Island study (habitability/erosion); Schulte et al., 'Climate Change and the Evolution and Fate of the Tangier Islands,' PMC4675185; Wikipedia/U.S. Census 'Tangier, Virginia' (town land area 0.54 sq mi).
items.note: MOSTLY SOURCED, one figure to reconcile. 'Inhabited since the 1770s' confirmed (settlers arrived in the 1770s). '~15 feet/year' is within the documented range (sources cite 10–20 ft/year of unprotected-shoreline loss, ~9 acres/year total) — defensible but cite as a range or attribute to the USACE study. 'TWO SQUARE MILES' DISAGREES with the Census figure for the TOWN of Tangier (0.54 sq mi land). The ~2 sq mi figure likely refers to the larger island/marsh complex (Tangier + uplands + marsh) — recommend clarifying whether the book means the town, the inhabited uplands, or the whole island complex, and anchor to the 2015 USACE study which is the authoritative erosion source.

items.claim: Maryland oyster population 'roughly tripled since 2005'; five tributary-scale sanctuaries; hundreds of acres of rebuilt reef; tens of millions of oysters/year added (line 188).
items.source: Maryland DNR / Gov. Moore, 'Completion of Maryland's Five Tributary-Scale Oyster Restoration Sanctuaries,' Aug 26 2025 (https://news.maryland.gov/dnr/2025/08/26/governor-wes-moore-announces-completion-of-marylands-five-tributary-scale-oyster-restoration-sanctuaries/); Chesapeake Bay Foundation, 'Maryland Reports Oyster Population Has Tripled Since 2005' (https://www.cbf.org/news/maryland-reports-oyster-population-has-tripled-since-2005/).
items.note: SOURCED & matches book. Maryland's oyster population reached ~12 billion as of the 2024 assessment, more than triple the 2005 level; the five completed tributary-scale sanctuaries are Harris Creek, Little Choptank, Tred Avon, St. Mary's, and Manokin, totaling ~2,000 acres of restored habitat (this exceeds 'hundreds of acres' — the book understates rather than overstates). 'Tens of millions of oysters/year added' is consistent with NOAA/MD DNR restoration reporting. Mild date note: the 'tripled' figure is anchored to the 2024 assessment; cite that baseline year.

items.claim: State of Maryland filed for a federal disaster declaration for the oyster fishery; year not stated in the chapter (line 194).
items.source: Maryland Matters, 'Maryland requests disaster declaration for Chesapeake oyster fishery,' Feb 27 2026 (https://marylandmatters.org/2026/02/27/maryland-requests-disaster-declaration-for-chesapeake-oyster-fishery/); Gov. Moore letter to U.S. Commerce Secretary Lutnick, Feb 27 2026; Chesapeake Bay Foundation supporting statement.
items.note: SOURCED — SUPPLY THE YEAR: Gov. Wes Moore requested a federal fishery disaster declaration (under the Magnuson-Stevens Act) for the Chesapeake oyster fishery on February 27, 2026, in a letter to Commerce Secretary Howard Lutnick (Rep. Andy Harris had urged NOAA in early Feb 2026). The chapter should add 'in February 2026.' DATE-LOCK (today 2026-06-04): this is a live, very recent matter — verify before publication whether NOAA has acted on the request (granted/denied/pending), since the status will likely change.

items.claim: Reedville menhaden reduction fishery characterized as 'the largest single fishery on the East Coast' and the plant having 'employed several hundred people' (lines 111, 121).
items.source: NOAA Fisheries, Atlantic Menhaden species page (menhaden = largest landings by volume on the U.S. Atlantic coast, https://www.fisheries.noaa.gov/species/atlantic-menhaden); Center for Land Use Interpretation, 'Omega Protein Plant Port of Reedville' (https://clui.org/ludb/site/omega-protein-plant-port-reedville); National Fisherman / Omega Protein-Ocean Harvesters employment figures.
items.note: SOURCED & defensible. Atlantic menhaden is the largest fishery BY VOLUME/WEIGHT on the U.S. Atlantic coast (NOAA), and Reedville has historically been the #2 U.S. fishing port by landings (behind Kodiak, AK); so 'largest single fishery on the East Coast' is accurate as a by-volume claim — recommend the chapter say 'largest by volume' to be precise rather than implying largest by value. Employment: ~260 direct Omega employees + ~250 indirect = 'several hundred' confirmed. Both halves supported.

items.claim: Osprey reproductive failure in Bay tributaries linked to insufficient menhaden forage (line 113), hedged as 'suspicion among the people who watch ospreys.'
items.source: Watts, Academia et al., 'Demographic response of osprey within the lower Chesapeake Bay to fluctuations in menhaden stock,' Frontiers in Marine Science (Jan 2024, https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/marine-science/articles/10.3389/fmars.2023.1284462/full); Center for Conservation Biology, William & Mary (Bryan Watts); Audubon, 'Researchers Sound the Alarm Over the Chesapeake Bay's Ospreys.'
items.note: SOURCED — the hedge UNDERSTATES the evidence. Bryan Watts (Center for Conservation Biology, William & Mary) has published peer-reviewed research (Frontiers in Marine Science, Jan 2024) linking low osprey reproduction in the lower Chesapeake (e.g., Mobjack Bay, where a controlled supplemental-feeding experiment was run) to menhaden scarcity; 2023 surveys found reproduction below DDT-era lows. The chapter can upgrade 'suspicion among the people who watch ospreys' to cite the Watts/CCB Frontiers study directly. Note for balance: some fisheries scientists dispute the menhaden-causation link (Bay Journal, Dec 2024), so attribute the causal claim to Watts et al. rather than asserting it settled.

items.claim: Chesapeake watershed scale: ~64,000 square miles across six states and DC; Chesapeake Bay Program 'established 1983'; the Bay Program science advisory committee's review finding the cleanup 'not on track to meet its goals' (line 129, 131).
items.source: Chesapeake Bay Program (watershed ~64,000 sq mi; six states — DE, MD, NY, PA, VA, WV — plus DC; first Chesapeake Bay Agreement 1983); STAC, 'A Comprehensive Evaluation of System Response' (CESR), May 2023 (https://www.chesapeake.org/stac/cesr/); Chesapeake Bay Program / USGS press releases on lagging progress.
items.note: SOURCED & matches book. Watershed ~64,000 sq mi across six states + DC confirmed; the six states are Delaware, Maryland, New York, Pennsylvania, Virginia, West Virginia. Chesapeake Bay Program 'established 1983' correct (first Bay Agreement, 1983). The 'not on track to meet its goals' finding is confirmed by the STAC CESR report (May 2023), which found nonpoint-source (agricultural) pollution reductions insufficient to meet the Bay TMDL/water-quality goals. PRECISION FLAG: the CESR is described in the chapter as a '40-year review' but it was framed by STAC as a ~4-year evaluation effort (concluding ~2023, ~40 years after the 1983 founding); reword to avoid implying a formal '40-year review' document if that is not the title.

items.claim: Norway sovereign-wealth-fund / existence-proof claims previewed (lines 232-236): finite seabed resource acquired late 1960s-early 1970s; fund held in trust with draw limits; kept intact 'more than thirty years.'
items.note: OUT-OF-SCOPE FOR Ch3 SOURCING by design. These are a forward-preview of Ch4's Norway/Government Pension Fund Global material; the citation-evidence note itself flags that their canonical sourcing belongs to Ch4. Action for the author/editor: confirm Ch4 actually carries and cites these claims (Norwegian petroleum discovery ~1969 Ekofisk; fund established 1990, first deposit 1996; ~3-4% spending/'fiscal' rule), so that Ch3 does not assert them as its own unsourced facts. Verify the Ch4 numbers there; do not lock them as Ch3 citations.

items.claim: Ekofisk discovery: oil struck on Norwegian Block 2/4 'In 1969, three days before Christmas' — opening scene date/locator.
items.source: Norwegian Petroleum Museum / Industriminne, 'The discovery which changed Norway – Ekofisk' (ekofisk.industriminne.no); Norsk Hydro company history, '1969: Ekofisk – a Christmas surprise' (hydro.com); Wikipedia 'Ekofisk oil field' (Phillips Petroleum, Block 2/4).
items.note: Block 2/4 and Phillips Petroleum confirmed. 'Three days before Christmas' = Dec 22, 1969; sources record the well being abandoned/confirmed Dec 23 with the find reported to the Ministry's oil office that day and Norwegian authorities confirming around Christmas Eve. The Dec-22-to-24 window is accurate; if the author wants a hard date, 'two to three days before Christmas' is the defensible phrasing. Note 25 Oct 1969 is sometimes cited as the geological-discovery date; the Christmas framing refers to confirmation/reporting.

items.claim: Alberta Heritage Savings Trust Fund (established 1976) was 'repeatedly drawn down for general-revenue support during oil-price downturns' and growth was 'capped at a small fraction of what Norway's discipline allowed.'
items.source: Alberta Heritage Savings Trust Fund historical timeline (open.alberta.ca); Fraser Institute, 'Repairing Alberta's Heritage Fund for the Long-Term'; The Canadian Encyclopedia, 'Alberta Heritage Savings Trust Fund'; Wikipedia.
items.note: Established 1976 under Premier Lougheed (initial $1.5B + 30% of non-renewable resource revenue). Confirmed: dedicated contributions ended in 1986/87; nearly all fund earnings transferred to the General Revenue Fund; real value eroded by inflation. Fraser Institute: 1980-2014 resource revenue ~$190B but the fund was only ~$17.3B in 2014 — directly supports the 'capped at a small fraction' claim. Characterization is sound.

items.claim: Alaska Permanent Fund (established 1976) 'captured petroleum royalties for distribution as annual citizen dividends rather than long-term sovereign capitalization.'
items.source: Alaska Permanent Fund Corporation, 'History' (apfc.org); Alaska Constitution Art. IX §15 (1976); EIA 'Today in Energy' id=21052.
items.note: Established 1976 by constitutional amendment; minimum 25% of mineral royalties set aside; Permanent Fund Dividend first paid 1982. Characterization (royalties routed to citizen dividends rather than Norway-style sovereign capitalization) is accurate as a contrast. Minor nuance: the APF does retain a large invested corpus (~$89B as of Apr 2026); the dividend is paid from earnings, not the whole royalty stream — but the author's contrast (dividend model vs. perpetual-capitalization model) holds.

items.claim: Norway White Paper in the early 1970s; Government Petroleum Fund formally established 1990; renamed Government Pension Fund Global in 2006.
items.source: Norges Bank Investment Management, 'About the fund' (nbim.no); Government Pension Fund Act No. 123 of 21 Dec 2005 (effective 1 Jan 2006); Wikipedia 'Government Pension Fund of Norway'.
items.note: Petroleum Fund established by legislation 1990; first capital transfer 1996 (NOK ~1.98B). Renamed Government Pension Fund Global effective 1 Jan 2006 (Act of 21 Dec 2005). The 1971 'ten oil commandments' (Storting) is the usual early-1970s policy anchor for the 'White Paper in the early 1970s' reference; author should confirm whether the chapter means the 1971 ten commandments specifically or a later White Paper.

items.claim: Statoil renamed Equinor in 2018.
items.source: Equinor press release '15mar2018-statoil' and '16may2018-changes-name-equinor' (equinor.com); Wikipedia 'Equinor'.
items.note: Announced 15 Mar 2018; approved by AGM 15 May 2018; name change effective 16 May 2018. Confirmed.

items.claim: Fund holds 'more than two trillion dollars in assets' (deep-pass §9 gap #1).
items.source: Norges Bank Investment Management Annual Report 2025 (nbim.no): end-2025 value NOK 21,268 billion (~$2.1T); CNBC 27 Feb 2026; Wikipedia (over US$2T as of May 2026).
items.note: TRUE as of today (2026-06-04): fund crossed US$2 trillion. 'More than two trillion' is currently accurate but date-sensitive — relock the figure at submission with a dated NBIM source (e.g., 'NOK 21.3 trillion / ~$2.1T at end-2025, NBIM'). Value fluctuates with markets and the NOK/USD rate; cite as-of a date.

items.claim: Per-citizen fund value 'roughly three hundred and sixty thousand dollars for every person' (derived figure, deep-pass §9 gap #1).
items.source: NBIM 'The fund's value' (nbim.no): ~NOK 3.8 million per registered person end-2025; Wikipedia: 'over US$390,000 per Norwegian citizen' as of May 2026.
items.note: The book's $360,000 is now LOW. Current figure is ~$390,000/citizen (≈NOK 3.8M ÷ population ~5.5–5.6M). $360K likely reflects an earlier fund value. Relock at submission: show the arithmetic ($2.1T ÷ ~5.5M ≈ $380K–$390K) and date-stamp it, or hedge to 'roughly $380,000.' Flag for date-lock.

items.claim: Fiscal rule adopted in 2001 limiting annual withdrawals to expected real return 'roughly three percent'; capital never drawn down (deep-pass §9 gap #3).
items.source: Norwegian Ministry of Finance, 'The Norwegian Fiscal Policy Framework' (regjeringen.no); Norges Bank speech 'Does Norway save too much?' (2018); NHH 'When Norway rewrote the fiscal rule'.
items.note: Confirmed: handlingsregelen adopted 2001; transfers limited to expected real return; originally set at 4%, formally revised DOWN to 3% in spring 2017 (Thøgersen/Mork commissions). So '3%' is correct for the current rule, '4%' for 2001–2017. Author should ensure the prose reflects that 3% is the post-2017 figure (the 2001 rule used 4%). Principal not drawn down is correct — only expected real return is spent.

items.claim: Norway 'generates more income from investment returns today than the underlying oil and gas production generates in extraction revenue' (deep-pass §9 gap #4).
items.source: EITI, 'Norway: Revenue from oil fund now exceeds revenue from oil' (eiti.org); Fortune (2025) and CNBC (Feb 2026) reporting on fund returns (~$248B profit in the strong 2025 year).
items.note: Confirmed by EITI: for several recent years, annual fund investment returns have exceeded petroleum sales revenue. Caveat: this holds 'over time / in strong-return years' and reverses in down-market years — the claim is true on a multi-year basis but is not a fixed structural certainty every single year. Consider softening to 'in recent years' / 'now routinely' to be defensible across market cycles.

items.claim: Government-succession list showing the 2001 fiscal rule held across administrations: Stoltenberg I (Labour) → Bondevik II center-right → Stoltenberg II Labour → Solberg Conservative → Støre Labour-Centre (deep-pass §9 gap #3).
items.source: Norwegian government, 'Norwegian Prime Ministers since 1814' (regjeringen.no); Britannica 'List of prime ministers of Norway'; Wikipedia bios (Stoltenberg, Bondevik, Solberg, Støre).
items.note: Sequence confirmed: Stoltenberg I (Labour, Mar 2000–Oct 2001, adopted the rule) → Bondevik II (centre-right coalition, 2001–2005) → Stoltenberg II (Labour, 2005–2013) → Solberg (Conservative coalition, 2013–2021) → Støre (Labour–Centre coalition, 2021–). Matches the chapter exactly. (Note: Støre's coalition lost its Centre partner in 2024–25 and Stoltenberg returned as finance minister in 2025 — does not affect the cross-administration-continuity point.)

items.claim: State 'captures roughly seventy to eighty cents of every dollar of net petroleum wealth' via taxation, production licenses, Equinor ownership, fund contributions (deep-pass §9 gap #2).
items.source: Norwegian Petroleum (norskpetroleum.no), 'The Petroleum Tax System' and 'The government's revenues'; PwC Norway corporate tax summary.
items.note: The 78% marginal tax rate (22% ordinary corporate + special petroleum tax to a combined 78%) is the well-documented anchor and sits squarely in the '70–80 cents' band. The broader 'net petroleum wealth' framing (tax + SDFI/State's Direct Financial Interest + Equinor majority state ownership + production licenses) means the state's total take of net cash flow is commonly cited around 78%+; the claim is sound. For a hard cite, lead with the 78% marginal rate (norskpetroleum.no) and characterize the rest as the broader take. Minor: 'production licenses' as a revenue lever is really the SDFI/state participation — author may want to name SDFI.

items.claim: Enforcement-mechanism claims: NBIM run at arm's length; public ethics-council divestment decisions; fiscal rule coupled to quarterly reporting; Storting structural change requires a consensus supermajority.
items.source: NBIM governance pages (nbim.no); Council on Ethics for the GPFG (etikkradet.no); Norwegian Ministry of Finance fiscal-framework page (regjeringen.no).
items.note: Mostly sound: NBIM manages the fund at arm's length under the Ministry of Finance / central bank; the independent Council on Ethics issues public divestment/exclusion recommendations; the fund reports quarterly. CAUTION on 'consensus supermajority': the fiscal rule and fund framework rest on a broad, durable cross-party POLITICAL consensus, but I did not find an authoritative source for a formal constitutional/statutory SUPERMAJORITY requirement to change the fund's structure — it is governed by ordinary legislation backed by strong convention. Recommend the author reframe to 'broad cross-party consensus' rather than asserting a formal supermajority rule, unless a specific statute can be cited. Flag this sub-claim for author/source confirmation.

items.claim: Norwegian extraction pace is 'deliberately limited, despite recurring industry pressure to accelerate.'
items.note: Defensible as a characterization (the 'moderate pace / ten oil commandments' tradition is real and widely discussed) but no single authoritative source was pulled in budget. Where to look next: Norway's 1971 'ten oil commandments' (Stortinget), Ministry of Energy production-management White Papers, and IEA/academic discussions of Norway's depletion-rate policy. Low-to-medium priority; lightly hedge or cite a Ministry White Paper.

items.claim: Catalog of petroleum applications for which 'no renewable alternative yet exists' (medical-grade plastics, aviation jet fuel, specialty petrochemistry, asphalt, carbon-fiber and lubricant chemistries).
items.source: U.S. DOE Alternative Fuels Data Center, 'Sustainable Aviation Fuel'; EIA 'U.S. sustainable aviation fuel production' (id=65204); literature on bio-based polymers (PE/PP/PET substitutes).
items.note: 'No renewable alternative YET EXISTS' is too absolute and is contradicted in the strict sense: sustainable aviation fuel (SAF) and bio-based polymers DO exist — they are just not yet scalable/cost-competitive (SAF is <1% of global jet fuel). The defensible claim is 'no SCALABLE or cost-competitive renewable alternative yet exists at industrial volume,' not 'no alternative exists.' Recommend the author rephrase to the scalability framing; as written, a hostile reader can falsify it with one SAF citation.

items.claim: North Sea drilling 'documentary history of oil spills, drilling-fluid discharges, decommissioned-platform seabed disturbances, and cumulative carbon emissions,' and combustion emissions 'persist in the atmosphere for centuries.'
items.source: On atmospheric CO2 lifetime: IPCC AR6 WG1 (a substantial fraction of emitted CO2 persists for centuries to millennia); on North Sea environmental impacts: OSPAR Commission discharge/decommissioning reports.
items.note: The 'CO2 persists for centuries' claim is well-grounded (IPCC: ~15–40% of a CO2 pulse remains after centuries; tail persists for millennia) — defensible. The specific North Sea spill/discharge/decommissioning 'documentary history' is real and documented by OSPAR but was not individually verified in budget; low risk. Recommend citing IPCC AR6 for the atmospheric-persistence clause and OSPAR for the discharge history if a cite is wanted.

items.claim: Nigeria 'a major oil producer since the 1950s' and Niger Delta reserves have generated 'cumulatively, hundreds of billions of dollars of petroleum revenue over six decades' (deep-pass §9 gap #5).
items.source: NEITI (Nigeria Extractive Industries Transparency Initiative) figures reported via Nairametrics (2023): ~$583 billion since independence (1960); $764.52 billion for the oil & gas sector 1999–2021; Wikipedia 'Petroleum industry in Nigeria' (first export 1958, Oloibiri).
items.note: Confirmed. First commercial oil/export 1958 (Oloibiri) — 'since the 1950s' is accurate. 'Hundreds of billions over six decades' is well-supported: NEITI ~$583B since independence (government revenue) — squarely in the hundreds-of-billions range. Cite NEITI. (If the author means total petroleum value rather than government revenue, the figure is higher; specify which.)

items.claim: Revenue routing: much of Nigeria's oil revenue flowed through Shell, Chevron, ExxonMobil, Total into financial systems outside Nigeria, and a substantial fraction reaching the state was 'captured by political elites in Abuja, Lagos, and the international accounts routinely documented in subsequent corruption prosecutions.'
items.note: Broadly consistent with the public record (Abacha-loot recovery cases, Malabu/OPL 245 Shell-Eni prosecution, repeated asset-repatriation agreements) but the specific routing claim was not pinned to a single authoritative source in budget. Where to look next: US DOJ / Swiss / UK asset-recovery filings on the Abacha funds; the OPL 245 (Malabu) prosecution record; Human Rights Watch and Chatham House reports on Nigerian oil-revenue capture. Medium priority — defensible but should be cited or hedged given it names companies and alleges elite capture.

items.claim: International-architecture passage: tax-treaty structures legitimizing transfer-pricing into low-disclosure jurisdictions, financial-secrecy intermediation, concession terms inherited from pre-independence arrangements (Pistor/Code-of-Capital seam).
items.note: This is an interpretive/analytical passage, not a discrete factual claim — per the deep-pass note it is a Pistor 'Code of Capital' seam to either develop-with-citation or cut. If retained, ground it in Katharina Pistor, 'The Code of Capital' (2019) and/or Tax Justice Network reporting on transfer-pricing/financial-secrecy. Author decision: develop with citation or cut. Not web-resolvable as a single fact.

items.claim: Hanging of Ken Saro-Wiwa in 1995; the Ogoni crisis of the 1990s; subsequent militant insurgencies (deep-pass §9 gap #6).
items.source: Britannica, 'Ken Saro-Wiwa'; Wikipedia 'Ken Saro-Wiwa'; UNPO 20-year anniversary record.
items.note: Confirmed: Ken Saro-Wiwa and eight others (the 'Ogoni Nine') executed by hanging on 10 November 1995 under the Abacha regime. MOSOP-led nonviolent campaign; later Niger Delta militancy (e.g., MEND) is historical record. Low-risk historical fact.

items.claim: International response to the 1995 executions: 'diplomatic protests, the temporary suspension of Nigeria from the Commonwealth of Nations' (deep-pass §9 gap #6).
items.source: Britannica and Wikipedia 'Ken Saro-Wiwa'; Commonwealth records.
items.note: Confirmed: Nigeria was suspended from the Commonwealth one day after the executions (11 Nov 1995) and remained suspended until 1999 (reinstated after the return to civilian rule). 'Temporary suspension' is accurate; the author could quantify as 'suspended for roughly three and a half years (1995–1999)' if precision is wanted.

items.claim: Resource-curse framing of Nigeria generally — entirely uncited; no Sachs-Warner / Ross nod (deep-pass §9 gap #7).
items.note: This is a positioning/attribution gap, not a checkable fact. The canonical literature to cite if the author wants to ground it: Sachs & Warner, 'Natural Resource Abundance and Economic Growth' (1995/2001); Michael L. Ross, 'The Oil Curse' (2012). Author must decide whether to add these citations to anchor the resource-curse framing.

items.claim: U.S. Social Security architecture: pay-as-you-go; surplus invested in U.S. Treasury bonds whose proceeds the government spent on general operations; 'trust fund' as an accounting record of intra-government debt rather than a compounding external pool (deep-pass §9 gap #9).
items.source: Social Security Administration, 'The Social Security Trust Funds and the Federal Budget' (ssa.gov/history/BudgetTreatment.html); Wikipedia 'Social Security Trust Fund'.
items.note: Confirmed by SSA's own history: pay-as-you-go; surplus invested in non-marketable special-issue Treasury obligations; those balances are intragovernmental debt (one part of government owing another), not an externally invested compounding pool. The 1935 Act required surplus to be invested in U.S. government obligations. Characterization is accurate — strong contrast point against Norway's external-pool model.

items.claim: U.S. Social Security 'designed in 1935, in the middle of the Great Depression,' with the pay-as-you-go form chosen for immediate benefit delivery and political reachability (deep-pass §9 gap #9).
items.source: Social Security Act of 1935; SSA history (ssa.gov/history).
items.note: Confirmed: Social Security Act signed by FDR August 1935, during the Great Depression. The 'pay-as-you-go for immediate benefit delivery' characterization is broadly accurate (the system shifted decisively toward PAYG with the 1939 amendments, which accelerated benefits and reduced reserve buildup). Minor nuance: the original 1935 design contemplated a larger reserve; the 1939 amendments cemented the PAYG character. Author may want to note '1935, refined in 1939' for precision.

items.claim: Bismarck's Germany: the '1889 Old Age and Disability Insurance system' had a 'partially-funded character' that 'survived until the post-WWI hyperinflation of the early 1920s' (deep-pass §9 gap #8; C-1 cut candidate).
items.source: Social Security Administration, 'Otto von Bismarck' (ssa.gov/history/ottob.html); Deutschlandmuseum '1889 origin of statutory retirement insurance'.
items.note: Confirmed in part: Germany adopted the first national old-age/disability insurance in May 1889 (cost split employer/employee plus state subsidy — i.e., partially funded). The general claim that 1920s hyperinflation wiped out pension reserves/savings is well-supported. CAVEAT: I did NOT find an authoritative source pinning the specific 'partially-funded reserve survived until the early-1920s hyperinflation destroyed it' chain to one citation — the reserve-destruction mechanism is plausible and consistent but under-sourced. If retained (deep-pass marks it a cut candidate), the author should source the reserve-destruction claim specifically; otherwise hedge or cut.

items.claim: Climate-externality incidence: atmospheric carbon from combusted Norwegian petroleum 'falls disproportionately on populations in tropical and subtropical regions — Bangladesh, the Sahel, low-lying Pacific island states.'
items.source: IPCC AR6 WG2 (2022), Summary for Policymakers — regional differential climate-impact vulnerability (low-lying coastal/SIDS, Sahel, South Asia among most exposed/least adaptive-capacity regions).
items.note: Well-grounded as a general claim: IPCC AR6 WG2 documents disproportionate climate-impact burden on low-latitude, low-income, low-adaptive-capacity regions including small-island states, the Sahel, and South Asia. Cite IPCC AR6 WG2 SPM. The specific causal attribution to 'Norwegian' petroleum is rhetorical (any combusted fossil carbon contributes to the same global pool) — fine as framing, not a per-source incidence calculation.

items.claim: Unnamed-but-present literature attributions: fund logic is Hotelling rent-capture; the fund is the Hartwick rule in operation; strong-sustainability is Daly; intergenerational obligation is Parfit (deep-pass §6 positioning gaps).
items.note: Positioning/attribution decisions, not checkable facts. Canonical anchors if the author chooses to ground them: Harold Hotelling, 'The Economics of Exhaustible Resources' (1931); John Hartwick, 'Intergenerational Equity and the Investing of Rents from Exhaustible Resources' (1977); Herman Daly on strong sustainability (e.g., 'Beyond Growth,' 1996); Derek Parfit, 'Reasons and Persons' (1984) on obligations to future generations. Author must decide whether to name these or leave the substance unattributed.

items.claim: Libby, MT documented costs 'exceed four billion dollars' against ~$100M lifetime revenue (40:1 cost-to-revenue ratio).
items.note: No authoritative source corroborates a documented $4B societal/health/cleanup cost for Libby. Documented public figures are far smaller and of different kinds: EPA direct cleanup spending ~$600M+ (as of 2018); W.R. Grace settlement $250M (largest in Superfund history) per DOJ #08-194 (2008-03-11); plus $18.5M state natural-resource damages. The '$4 billion' appears in unrelated contexts (W.R. Grace allegedly shielded ~$4B in assets per a Congressional hearing; a separate ~$3B Grace asbestos trust fund). The 40:1 ratio rests on an uncited numerator. RECOMMEND: either cite the specific cost study or demote the figure / reframe as an order-of-magnitude estimate with disclosed components.

items.claim: Libby mortality: 400+ residents dead; ~3,000 diagnosed with lung abnormalities; asbestosis mortality 40-80x national average.
items.source: ATSDR, Mortality in Libby, Montana (reviews 1979-1998 / updated 1979-2011); Naik et al., 'Mortality from asbestos-associated disease in Libby, Montana 1979-2011,' J Exposure Sci Environ Epidemiol (2017), doi:10.1038/jes.2016.18 (694 decedents with >=1 asbestos-related cause, 1979-2011); Antao et al., Environ Health Perspect 115(4):579 (2007).
items.note: The 40-80x figure is well-sourced: ATSDR found asbestosis mortality 40-80x higher than expected vs MT/US (lung-cancer mortality ~20-30% higher). '~400 deaths' aligns with the ATSDR/early-study count for the 1990s window; longer windows give 694 asbestos-related decedents (1979-2011) — so 400+ is conservative/defensible. The '~3,000 diagnosed with lung abnormalities' is the weakest sub-figure: the medical-screening cohort was ~7,307 persons with pleural/interstitial abnormalities found in a subset — pin the exact 'abnormalities' count to the ATSDR screening report or Antao 2007 before asserting 3,000.

items.claim: Libby mine produced ~80% of world vermiculite; ~$100M lifetime revenue; Zonolite in ~35 million American homes; W.R. Grace identified contamination as early as 1963.
items.source: US EPA Libby Asbestos Superfund Site Profile (cumulis.epa.gov, id=0801744) — mine 'may have produced 80 percent of the world's supply of vermiculite'; Grace bought Zonolite operations 1963 and knew vermiculite was asbestos-contaminated; US EPA, 'Protect Your Family from Asbestos-Contaminated Vermiculite Insulation' (EPA internal estimate: up to 35 million homes/schools/businesses).
items.note: 80% world vermiculite: supported by EPA ('may have produced 80%'). 1963 Grace acquisition + knowledge-of-contamination: supported by EPA + DOJ. 35 million homes: supported as an EPA internal estimate (widely cited, but it is an estimate of homes containing the insulation, not a precise count — flag as 'as many as 35 million'). The '~$100M lifetime revenue' figure was NOT found in any authoritative source — UNSOURCED; if the 40:1 ratio depends on it, both numerator and denominator need a cite. NB distinction: '80% of WORLD vermiculite' (EPA) vs. the commonly cited '70-80% of US vermiculite insulation' — keep the 'world' framing tied to the EPA Superfund profile language.

items.claim: EPA's public-health-emergency declaration for Libby was the FIRST ever for a man-made disaster.
items.source: US EPA press release, 'EPA Announces Public Health Emergency in Libby, Montana' (2009-06-17): 'This is the first time EPA has made a determination under the Comprehensive Environmental Response, Compensation, and Liability Act (CERCLA) that conditions at a site constitute a public health emergency.'
items.note: The authoritative claim of firstness is narrower and more precise than the book's: it is the first (and to date only) public-health-emergency determination under CERCLA/Superfund — not framed by EPA as 'first ever for a man-made disaster.' RECOMMEND rephrasing to 'the first public-health-emergency determination in EPA Superfund history' (2009-06-17) to match the source exactly; the 'man-made disaster' gloss is the author's characterization and is defensible but not the source's language.

items.claim: Deepwater Horizon: $150 billion total economic losses (aggregate from academic, insurance-industry, and federal sources) — the denominator of the 40% recovery ratio.
items.note: No authoritative source produces a ~$150B aggregate total-economic-loss figure. Documented estimates are far smaller and partial: 7-year fisheries present-value loss ~$8.7B (Sumaila et al., Can J Fish Aquat Sci 2011/2012); U.S. Travel Assoc. tourism impact 'could exceed ~$23B' over 3 years; a 2010-2020 modeled loss of ~$2.3B output. BP's own final all-in cost estimate was $61.6B (June 2016). The $150B denominator drives the headline '40% recovery' ratio and is the load-bearing unsourced figure for the Deepwater section. RECOMMEND naming the specific aggregation or replacing with a defended range.

items.claim: Deepwater Horizon: BP paid ~$60-65B total ($21B fines/settlements + $20B compensation fund); ~$60B Gulf revenue in five years; Macondo projected 40-60M barrels worth $3-4B.
items.source: BP final cost estimate of $61.6 billion (June 2016, widely reported / BP financial disclosures); BP-US $20.8B settlement approved April 2016 (DOJ); 2015 BP announcement of settlement 'up to $18.7B' with federal/state/local claims (bp.com press release).
items.note: The BP ~$60-65B all-in figure is SOURCED ($61.6B BP final estimate, June 2016) and the ~$20B Gulf-states settlement is sourced. The internal split into '$21B fines + $20B compensation fund' is roughly consistent but should be pinned to BP's own cost breakdown. The '~$60B Gulf revenue in five years' and 'Macondo 40-60M barrels worth $3-4B' projections were NOT independently corroborated — flag those two sub-figures as UNSOURCED within an otherwise sourced item.

items.claim: Deepwater Horizon: 4.9 million barrels released over 87 days; 11 workers killed.
items.source: U.S. federal government estimate of 4.9 million barrels total discharge (Deepwater Horizon oil spill); well flowed 87 days (April 20 - July 15, 2010); 11 workers killed in the explosion. Corroborated across US EPA (Deepwater Horizon enforcement page), NOAA, and the federal record.
items.note: Fully corroborated by the federal record. The 4.9M-barrel figure is the U.S. government estimate (some sources cite a lower 3.19M 'released to ocean' net-of-recovery figure — the 4.9M total-discharge number is the standard federal estimate and is what the book uses). Safe as written; cite NOAA or the US v. BP record inline.

items.claim: Deepwater Horizon: BP-Mexico 2018 settlement ~$25 million, 'three orders of magnitude smaller' than U.S. settlements per mile of coastline.
items.source: BuzzFeed News (Janowitz), 'A Confidential $25 Million Settlement Cleared BP Of Polluting Mexico's Water' (2018); Vice News, 'BP Paid Mexico $25.5M After the Deepwater Horizon Oil Spill' — confidential settlement signed by the Peña Nieto government, 2018.
items.note: The ~$25M (precisely $25.5M) Mexico settlement is SOURCED. The magnitude contrast holds: $25.5M vs. BP's >$60B U.S. obligations is roughly 2,400x (~3 orders of magnitude), so 'three orders of magnitude smaller' is defensible. CAVEAT: the 'per mile of coastline' normalization is the book's own framing, not in the sources — present it as the author's calculation, not a sourced figure. Sources are journalistic (not GAO/primary); for a book consider citing the underlying settlement document if obtainable.

items.claim: Deepwater Horizon: tourism revenue losses >$7B over three years; property-value declines ~$12B.
items.note: Not corroborated as stated. The nearest authoritative tourism figure is the U.S. Travel Association estimate that Gulf-coast tourism impact 'could exceed ~$23B over three years' — which is LARGER than and inconsistent with the book's '>$7B,' suggesting the $7B and the $12B property-value figures come from a different (unnamed) source. RECOMMEND pinning both to their original studies or dropping; the inconsistency with the widely cited $23B tourism estimate is a reviewer-exposure point.

items.claim: Baotou: ~60% of world rare earths; ~10 tons wastewater per ton rare-earth oxide; remediation costs 'will exceed one hundred billion dollars'; industry spends <1% of revenue on remediation; $10-12B annual Chinese rare-earth revenue (Baotou ~half).
items.source: Partial support only: tailings/wastewater rate corroborated by 'Environmental impacts of rare earth production,' PMC8929459 ('flotation tailings generated at rates of tens of tons per ton of rare earth oxide'); USGS rare-earth data (China ~69% of global production); Bayan Obo/Baotou ~40% of world reserves, ~half of China's output (SCMP; NS Energy).
items.note: MIXED. (a) '~10 tons wastewater per ton oxide' is conservative/supported (literature says tens of tons of tailings per ton). (b) '~60% of world rare earths' is imprecise: USGS puts CHINA at ~69% of global production; Baotou/Bayan Obo is ~half of China's output (~35% of world). Read as China's share it is roughly right (~60-69%); read as Baotou-specific it is too high — disambiguate. (c) The '$100 billion remediation cost' is the HIGHEST-RISK Baotou figure and is UNSOURCED — no authoritative source found. (d) '<1% of revenue on remediation' and the '$10-12B annual revenue' figures were NOT corroborated. The named journals (Environmental Pollution; Ecotoxicology & Environ. Safety; Science of the Total Environment) without specific papers is confirmed citation-theater — a reviewer will press. RECOMMEND pin specific papers or demote the $100B figure.

items.claim: Social Security: $108 trillion foregone-return figure ('largest cost severance in human history') — equity-index counterfactual.
items.note: HIGHEST-RISK numbers-are-invented exposure in the chapter. No source produces a $108T figure. The nearest authoritative counterfactual (Brookings, 'How would investing in equities have affected the Social Security trust fund?') estimates that ~40% equity allocation from 1984 would have left reserves ~$3.8T vs. actual ~$2.8T at end-2015 — a gap of ~$1T, two orders of magnitude below $108T. The $108T is an own-model output, highly sensitive to equity-return series, contribution timing, and drawdown assumptions. RECOMMEND either (i) full methodology disclosure (return index, period, contribution/drawdown schedule, real vs. nominal) as a footnoted band, or (ii) demotion to an explicitly labeled illustrative counterfactual. Do not present as a 'documented' figure.

items.claim: 2008: ~10 million households received foreclosure filings 2008-2012; ~5 million completed foreclosures; TARP $700B; HAMP reached a small fraction of eligible applicants with administrative failures documented in 'multiple GAO reports.'
items.source: TARP authorized up to $700B (EESA 2008); GAO TARP/HAMP oversight (e.g., GAO-10-531; GAO-24-107033 TARP lifetime cost); foreclosure-volume data (CoreLogic / RealtyTrac as compiled in GAO and the FCIC final report ch.22). ~4 million completed foreclosures Sept 2008-Sept 2012 (CoreLogic, cited widely).
items.note: TARP $700B and the HAMP-underperformance/GAO-criticism narrative are SOURCED (GAO issued multiple HAMP oversight reports). Foreclosure magnitudes are broadly supported but the book's specific counts run slightly high: completed foreclosures Sept2008-Sept2012 are usually given as ~4 million (book says ~5M) and filings vary by source. RECOMMEND: cite the specific CoreLogic/RealtyTrac series for the filing/completion counts and name 1-2 specific GAO HAMP reports (the 'multiple GAO reports' phrase is true but should be pinned — e.g., GAO-12-783 / SIGTARP quarterly reports) rather than left as a general attribution.

items.claim: GoFundMe: 438,000 medical campaigns / >$2B / 21.7M donations (2016-2020); only 12% reached goals (88% failure); 16% received zero donations; CEO acknowledged the platform was never designed as healthcare financing; 25-fold growth in medical campaigns 2011-2020.
items.source: Kenworthy N, Igra M, 'Medical Crowdfunding and Disparities in Health Care Access in the United States, 2016-2020,' American Journal of Public Health 112(3):491-498 (March 2022), doi:10.2105/AJPH.2021.306617 (437,596 campaigns; >$2B from 21.7M donations; <12% met goals; 16.1% zero donations). 25-fold-growth: KFF Health News, 'GoFundMe Has Become a Health Care Utility' (2024), citing 2020 ~200,000 medical campaigns = 25x the 2011 number.
items.note: Core figures SOURCED and precise: 437,596 campaigns (book's '438,000' rounds correctly), 21.7M donations, <12% met goals (= ~88% did not), 16.1% zero donations — all from Kenworthy & Igra, AJPH 2022. The 25-fold 2011-2020 growth is sourced separately (KFF Health News 2024). The CEO acknowledgment that GoFundMe was 'never designed' as a healthcare-financing system is widely quoted (CEO Rob Solomon) but should be pinned to the specific interview/quote. Map the chapter's footnote to AJPH 2022 + KFF 2024.

items.claim: GoFundMe racial disparity: campaigns by individuals with distinctively African American or Hispanic surnames raise substantially less and are less likely to meet goals, controlling for geography/time.
items.source: Best peer-reviewed match: 'The role of race and ethnicity in health care crowdfunding: an exploratory analysis,' Health Affairs Scholar 2(3):qxae027 (2024), doi:10.1093/haschl/qxae027 (name+geography algorithm; Black/Hispanic associated with lower amounts raised and lower success rate vs. White organizers). Corroborating: Saleh et al., JMIR 2023, e51089; Silver et al., Soc Sci Med 2023 (PMC10916987).
items.note: SOURCED — the disparity finding is robust across several peer-reviewed papers. The book's exact methodology phrasing ('distinctively African American or Hispanic surnames') most closely matches name-inference approaches; if the chapter wants the single nameable paper using a surname-distinctiveness method, the Health Affairs Scholar 2024 paper (name+geography algorithm) is the cleanest cite. If the chapter's source is actually the J Econ Behav Organ 2023 'causes and consequences of medical crowdfunding' paper (which I could not fully verify uses the surname method), confirm which paper the author intends before locking the footnote.

items.claim: Katy Butler figures: $54 office visit / $461 pacemaker implantation / ~$12,000 hospital flat fee / ~$6,000 device manufacturer; 80-year-old caregiver, 80-hour weeks, five years.
items.source: Katy Butler, Knocking on Heaven's Door: The Path to a Better Way of Death (Simon & Schuster, 2013), ISBN 9781451641981.
items.note: The book exists and is the correct source, but the specific dollar figures ($54 / $461 / ~$12,000 / ~$6,000) and the '80-year-old caregiver, 80-hour weeks, five years' details are not web-resolvable — they require a PAGE-CITE to Butler's book (or her NYT Magazine essay 'What Broke My Father's Heart,' June 18, 2010). Author must supply page numbers. ALSO VERIFY a possible drift: published reviews describe Butler's FATHER as ~84 when the pacemaker was implanted and her MOTHER as the caregiver; confirm the '80-year-old caregiver' attribution matches the book (which person, which age) before locking.

items.claim: Black Lung Benefits Program: $44 billion paid across half a century; Trust Fund ~$5.1 billion outstanding debt 'as of September 2024.'
items.source: $5.1B-as-of-Sept-2024: consistent with GAO / Brookings ('Putting the trust back in the Black Lung Disability Trust Fund') and CRS R45261 ('The Black Lung Program, the Black Lung Disability Trust Fund, and the Excise Tax on Coal'). Total-benefits and debt history: CRS R45261; GAO-18-351; GAO-19-622T.
items.note: The $5.1B-outstanding-debt-as-of-September-2024 figure is corroborated but is INHERENTLY DATE-LOCKED: today is 2026-06-04, so 'as of September 2024' is ~20 months stale and the balance has moved (federal projections cited debt rising toward ~$13B by 2050). Either keep the explicit 'as of September 2024' date stamp with a CRS/GAO cite, or relock to the most recent figure. The '$44 billion paid across half a century' total was NOT directly confirmed in search — pin it to CRS R45261 (which carries the historical benefit-payment totals) before asserting; treat as unsourced until that page-cite is in hand.

items.claim: Reclamation bonds cover '$4-6 billion less than actual reclamation cost.'
items.source: Appalachian Voices analysis (2021/2023): seven Eastern states' coal-reclamation liabilities ~$7.5-9.8B vs. ~$3.8B bonding available (gap ~$3.7-6B). Underlying federal source: GAO-18-305, 'Coal Mine Reclamation: Federal and State Agencies Face Challenges in Managing Billions in Financial Assurances' (2018).
items.note: Order-of-magnitude SOURCED: the $7.5-9.8B-liabilities vs ~$3.8B-bonding gap in seven Eastern states yields a shortfall of roughly $4-6B, matching the book. CAVEAT: the clean $4-6B gap number is from Appalachian Voices (advocacy org), not a direct GAO statement; GAO-18-305 documents the inadequacy and forfeiture data (450 forfeitures 2007-2016; 22% with insufficient assurances) but does not state a single '$4-6B' figure. RECOMMEND citing GAO-18-305 for the structural inadequacy and attributing the specific $4-6B gap to Appalachian Voices, or reframe as a national-scope estimate.

items.claim: The United States is the only wealthy nation whose healthcare-financing architecture produces the GoFundMe-as-default pattern at this scale (line 166).
items.note: Comparative 'only wealthy nation... at this scale' claim was not run to ground with a single authoritative comparative source. The underlying premise (US medical crowdfunding volume vastly exceeds peer nations) is supported in the medical-crowdfunding literature and journalism (KFF, Kenworthy), but the strong universal 'only' framing needs either a comparative study citation or softening to 'no other wealthy nation relies on medical crowdfunding at comparable scale.' RECOMMEND: look in Kenworthy's broader body of work or OECD health-financing comparisons; otherwise hedge the 'only' claim.

items.claim: Cost severance 'is the mechanism by which every extractive economy has ever operated' — Polanyi/Fraser fictitious-commodity / expropriation positioning.
items.source: Karl Polanyi, The Great Transformation (1944) — fictitious commodities (land, labor, money); Nancy Fraser, e.g., 'Behind Marx's Hidden Abode' (New Left Review 2014) / Cannibal Capitalism (2022) — expropriation vs. exploitation.
items.note: This is a THEORETICAL-POSITIONING gap, not a factual one — deep-pass flags Polanyi and Fraser as absent from the chapter despite the claim sitting squarely in their fictitious-commodity / expropriation tradition. Not web-researchable in the figure sense; the author must decide whether to anchor the universal 'every extractive economy' claim to Polanyi (fictitious commodities) and Fraser (expropriation) explicitly, or soften the universal quantifier. The canonical citations above are stable and safe to add; the author must confirm the framing alignment.

items.claim: 'Darity 2026' (line 240, informing TA §1.10 coercion boundary) is a real, citable forthcoming work rather than a placeholder.
items.note: Not web-verifiable as a citable work (a 2026 forthcoming item would not yet be reliably indexed, and no matching Darity 2026 was surfaced). The author must confirm whether 'Darity 2026' is a real forthcoming publication (with title/venue) or a placeholder to be resolved. If placeholder, either remove the inline cite or replace with the appropriate existing Darity work (e.g., Darity & Mullen, From Here to Equality, 2020, already cited elsewhere in the chapter).
