items.claim: McDowell County 'thirteen-year' life-expectancy gap vs the national average (¶32, ¶295)
items.source: CDC/USALEEP & IHME small-area life-expectancy data as reported in Bluefield Daily Telegraph, 'Deadly distinction: Life expectancy for males in McDowell County lowest in U.S.' (2016), and corroborated by West Virginia Watch (CDC 2021 data, Sept 26 2024). McDowell male life expectancy ~63.5 yrs vs U.S. male ~76.5 yrs; female ~71.5 vs ~81.2. Most recent county figure 66.3 yrs (2019–2021). For a primary cite use CDC National Center for Health Statistics U.S. Small-Area Life Expectancy Estimates Project (USALEEP).
items.note: Tier-0, load-bearing. The ~13-year gap is well-supported FOR MALES specifically (63.5 vs 76.5 = ~13). The all-population/county figure is closer to a ~10–12 year gap (county 66.3 vs U.S. ~77–78). Recommend the prose specify 'for men' if it currently implies an all-population thirteen-year gap; otherwise the figure reads slightly high for the combined-sex comparison. Prefer citing the CDC USALEEP primary dataset over the newspaper. TIME-SENSITIVE: life-expectancy figures update; date-lock to the CDC vintage cited.

items.claim: Darity's 'six-to-seven year Black-versus-white longevity gap' priced as a legacy effect (¶34)
items.source: Himmelstein KEW, Lawrence JA, Jahn JL, Ceasar JN, Morse M, Bassett MT, Wispelwey BP, Darity WA Jr, Venkataramani AS. 'Association Between Racial Wealth Inequities and Racial Disparities in Longevity Among US Adults and Role of Reparations Payments, 1992 to 2018.' JAMA Network Open. 2022;5(11):e2240519. doi:10.1001/jamanetworkopen.2022.40519. PMID 36342718.
items.note: FIGURE DISAGREEMENT to flag: this specific Darity-coauthored study reports a ~4-year median gap (Black 77.5 vs White 81.5), with the gap largely mediated by household wealth (survival did not differ significantly by race after wealth adjustment) — which is the exact 'priced as a legacy effect' point the book wants. The '6–7 year' figure is the HISTORICAL gap (7.6 yrs in 1970, 5.7 in 2000, ~3.6 by 2013 per NCHS), not this paper's headline number. If the book attributes a specific '6–7 year' figure to Darity, either (a) re-anchor to the ~4-year wealth-mediated figure from JAMA Netw Open 2022, or (b) cite the historical NCHS gap separately. Verify which number the prose intends.

items.claim: Appalachian reclamation bonds '$3.7 to $6 billion short' of the work required (¶30)
items.source: Appalachian Voices, mine-reclamation liability analysis (2021): seven Eastern states' reclamation liabilities of $7.5–$9.8 billion vs ~$3.8 billion in available bonding (appvoices.org/2023/10/19/gao-mine-reclamation; appvoices.org/2023/12/15). Corroborated by U.S. GAO, 'Coal Mine Reclamation: Federal and State Agencies Face Challenges in Managing Billions in Financial Assurances,' GAO-18-305 (2018) — ~$10.2B total financial assurances held as of 2017; self-bonds ~$1.2B; GAO recommends eliminating self-bonding.
items.note: SOURCED and figure CONFIRMS the book: $7.5–$9.8B liability minus $3.8B available = $3.7–$6.0B shortfall, exactly the book's '$3.7 to $6 billion short.' Ch2 cross-reference check: Ch2 carries the same figures ($7.5–$9.8B / $3.8B / $4–$6B gap) but per the Ch2 citation-evidence its only INLINE source is SMCRA (1977) the statute, NOT the dollar figures — so the public anchor (Appalachian Voices 2021 + GAO-18-305) needs to be added in Ch2 and cross-referenced here. TIME-SENSITIVE: bonding/liability figures are 2017–2021 vintage; date-lock.

items.claim: Bare-prose total-cost figures: Deepwater Horizon '$65 to $70 billion', Libby '$5 to $8 billion', Exxon Valdez '$7 to $10 billion' (¶46)
items.source: Deepwater: BP financial disclosures, 'BP Takes $1.7 Billion Charge... Costs Now Top $65B' (US News/AP, Jan 16 2018); Wikipedia/Deepwater Horizon litigation. Exxon Valdez: EVOS Trustee Council; total has 'at times been estimated at as much as US$7 billion' (cleanup ~$2.2–2.5B + $1.025B 1991 settlement + claims). Libby: U.S. DOJ press release #08-194 (2008) — W.R. Grace to pay $250M; EPA Superfund cleanup 'more than $600 million.'
items.note: MIXED with two CONTRADICTIONS. (1) Deepwater $65B is the documented floor (BP's own reported total 'topped $65B' by 2018); the '$70B' upper bound is plausible with later charges but cite a basis. (2) Exxon Valdez: '$7 billion' is the documented high-end estimate; the book's '$10 billion' ceiling EXCEEDS any public figure I can source — recommend trimming to ~$7B or sourcing the $10B basis. (3) Libby '$5 to $8 billion' is NOT supported by any public source I found — documented public costs are ~$600M EPA cleanup + $250M Grace reimbursement (low hundreds of millions, not billions). If $5–$8B reflects total societal/health/mortality cost, that basis must be named and sourced; as a bare 'total cost' it reads an order of magnitude high. ¶46 prose figures must be aligned to the convergence-table cell sourcing.

items.claim: Causal claim that 'much of the information was suppressed deliberately' regarding coal-industry information (¶82, ¶80)
items.source: Center for Public Integrity, Chris Hamby, 'Breathless and Burdened: Dying from Black Lung, Buried in Law' (2013–14; Pulitzer Prize for Investigative Reporting 2014) — documents Jackson Kelly PLLC withholding medical evidence favorable to miners' black-lung claims, and the Johns Hopkins/Dr. Paul Wheeler X-ray unit (Wheeler found zero cases of severe black lung in 1,500+ cases). Corroborated by NPR (Howard Berkes) and ABC News joint reporting; Johns Hopkins suspended the program in 2013.
items.note: SOURCEABLE but SCOPE-NARROW. The strong public record supports deliberate withholding/suppression of black-lung MEDICAL EVIDENCE in benefits litigation by industry lawyers and a hospital unit. If the book's '¶82 much of the information was suppressed deliberately' is scoped to black-lung disease evidence, this is solidly sourced — add the Hamby/CPI citation. If the claim is broader (all coal-industry cost/health information), it overreaches the documented record and should be narrowed to the litigation-evidence finding or softened. Recommend scoping + citing rather than dropping.

items.claim: Norway carbon tax introduced 1991 'one of the world's earliest'; petroleum extraction taxed at 'one of the highest rates'; ethics-council public divestments (¶219–221)
items.source: 1991 introduction: Statistics Norway (SSB) discussion papers; Norwegianpetroleum.no 'Emissions to air' — Norway among the first countries to introduce a CO2 tax, 1991. Petroleum-sector rate: NOK 761/tonne CO2 in 2023 (~US$69), among the highest sector rates globally. Ethics-council divestments: Norges Bank Investment Management / Council on Ethics for the Government Pension Fund Global (GPFG) public exclusion decisions (nbim.no observation-and-exclusion list).
items.note: All three sub-claims SOURCEABLE. '1991, one of the earliest' confirmed (Norway among Finland/Sweden/Denmark/Poland early adopters). 'One of the highest rates in any oil-producing economy' supported (petroleum CO2 tax ~NOK 761/t = ~US$69 in 2023, plus EU ETS). Ethics-council divestments are public record at the Council on Ethics / NBIM exclusion list — cite specific exclusion decisions if the book names particular divestments. TIME-SENSITIVE: the per-tonne rate updates annually; date-lock to 2023 vintage or update.

items.claim: Method 3 per-ton range '$420 to $13,100/ton, mid ~$2,500' and 'alpha-dominance regime 0.9–1.0' (¶143)
items.note: INTERNAL derivation (book's Technical Appendix cross-case sensitivity analysis); no external public source exists for these specific outputs and none should be expected. Nearest external anchor for a hostile reader: EPA Social Cost of Carbon — $190/t central (2% discount), up to ~$340/t at 1.5% (EPA 2023 draft); peer-reviewed Rennert et al., Nature 2022, mean ~$185/t with 5–95% range $44–$413/t. NONE of these approach the $13,100/ton ceiling. The deep-pass flag is correct: the $13,100 upper bound has no public anchor and will be challenged. Author action: either (a) show the derivation chain in the Technical Appendix explicitly so the ceiling is reconstructable, or (b) add a sentence noting it is a model-derived upper bound, not an empirical market/SCC figure. Cannot be web-sourced.

items.claim: 'Solar cells at three cents per kilowatt-hour' (¶76) and 'renewables already displacing coal at scale' (¶100)
items.source: Lazard, 'Levelized Cost of Energy+' v16.0 (April 2023): unsubsidized utility-scale solar PV LCOE range $24–$96/MWh (i.e., $0.024–$0.096/kWh); best-in-class low end ~$0.024/kWh. For 'displacing coal at scale': EIA Annual/Short-Term Energy Outlook coal-generation decline + Lazard LCOE crossover. (LBNL 'Utility-Scale Solar' report is an alternative primary anchor.)
items.note: 'Three cents per kWh' is SUPPORTABLE and conservative — it sits within Lazard's utility-scale range ($0.024–$0.096/kWh) and is above the best-in-class floor of ~2.4 cents, so it does not overstate. FLAG: 'solar cells at three cents' is a system-level LCOE figure, not a 'cell' cost; recommend phrasing as 'utility-scale solar electricity at ~3 cents/kWh (LCOE)' for accuracy. TIME-SENSITIVE: LCOE updates annually (and rose for the first time in 2023); date-lock to the Lazard vintage cited.

items.claim: Exxon Valdez '$5.5 million of oil spilled (product value)' (¶46)
items.note: No public source states a '$5.5 million product value' for the spilled crude; this appears to be the book's own derived figure. It is arithmetically plausible: ~11 million gallons ≈ 262,000 barrels × ~$20/bbl (1989 crude) ≈ $5.2–$5.5M. Author action: either show the back-of-envelope basis inline (volume × 1989 wellhead/spot price, citing EIA crude price series for 1989) or mark it as an illustrative computation. The rhetorical contrast (tiny product value vs ~$7B in damages) is the load-bearing point and survives even if framed as an estimate. Cannot be sourced to a single authority as stated.

items.claim: Contemporary mine-mouth coal price '$40 to $140 per ton depending on region and grade' (¶38)
items.source: U.S. EIA, Coal Markets weekly spot prices and Annual Coal Report 2024 (Table 28, average sales price by state/mine type). Recent regional spot prices: Powder River Basin ~$15/short ton, Illinois Basin ~$52, Central Appalachia ~$82/short ton (2024).
items.note: PARTIAL CONTRADICTION. The $40 floor and mid-range are plausible for Appalachian/Illinois Basin metallurgical and high-grade coal, but typical 2024 thermal mine-mouth/spot prices run ~$15 (PRB) to ~$82 (CAPP) per short ton — the '$140/ton' ceiling exceeds normal 2023–24 spot prices and reflects either metallurgical/coking coal or the 2022 energy-crisis spike. Author action: specify whether $140 is metallurgical coal or a peak-year figure, and cite EIA Annual Coal Report Table 28 / Coal Markets. TIME-SENSITIVE (highly volatile): date-lock the price range to a named EIA vintage; the 2022 spike has since receded.

items.claim: Bottom-up tier estimates: health $2–$4/ton, environment $1–$3/ton, community costs $5–$15/ton (¶28, ¶30, ¶32, ¶36)
items.note: Aggregate tier ranges are the book's own bottom-up construction; only the Black Lung sub-component carries an inline cite (per Ch2: Black Lung Disability Trust Fund $44B benefits / $4.6B debt / projected $15B by 2050). No single public source supplies these aggregated per-ton tier ranges, and none should be expected. For external defensibility, the strongest comparable public anchors are: EPA SCC (~$190/t CO2) for the climate/environment slice; NIOSH/CDC black-lung burden + Black Lung Trust Fund figures for the health slice; and Appalachian Voices/GAO reclamation-liability figures for the community/environment slice. Author action: footnote each tier to the underlying public component sources used to build it, so the 'conservative' framing is auditable. Cannot be sourced as a single aggregate figure.

items.claim: Method 2 Norway 'approximately fifty dollars' / '~$48' per barrel-of-oil-equivalent revealed-preference figure (¶141, ¶174)
items.note: INTERNAL derivation (Technical Appendix §3.6); the per-BOE revealed-preference value is a book computation, not a published figure. Public INPUT anchor available: Norway's petroleum-sector CO2 tax was NOK 761/tonne CO2 (~US$69) in 2023 (and ~NOK 543/t in 2021), among the world's highest — cite Statistics Norway / Norwegianpetroleum.no / Norwegian Tax Administration for the rate. But the '$48–$50/BOE' transformation is internal and unsourceable externally. TWO author actions: (1) show the per-tonne-CO2 → per-BOE derivation in the Technical Appendix with the public tax-rate input cited; (2) RESOLVE the internal $48-vs-$50 inconsistency flagged in deep-pass §8 — pick one figure and use it consistently in ¶141 and ¶174.

items.claim: 1960 mine-mouth coal price of 'roughly $4.50' (¶38, ¶80, table)
items.source: U.S. EIA, Annual Energy Review Table 7.9, 'Coal Prices, 1949–2011 (Dollars per Short Ton)' (https://www.eia.gov/totalenergy/data/annual/xls/stb0709.xls). 1960 nominal price: bituminous $4.71/short ton; all-coal total $4.83/short ton.
items.note: FIGURE DISAGREEMENT (minor). The EIA primary series gives the 1960 nominal mine-mouth price as ~$4.71 (bituminous) / $4.83 (all coal), not 'roughly $4.50.' The book's $4.50 undershoots the documented figure by ~5–7%. Recommend updating to 'roughly $4.70–$4.85 per ton (1960)' or softening to 'under five dollars,' and citing EIA AER Table 7.9. Anthracite was higher; if the book means a specific coal type other than bituminous, note it. Not time-sensitive (historical), but the citation should be added since it is a recurring anchor used at ¶38, ¶80, and in the table.

items.claim: Launch-cost trajectory (line 241): Space Shuttle era ~$30,000/kg to LEO; current commercial baseline ~$2,000-5,000/kg (Falcon 9/Falcon Heavy); ~$200-1,000/kg next decade contingent on Starship.
items.source: Harry W. Jones, 'The Recent Large Reduction in Space Launch Cost,' 48th International Conference on Environmental Systems (ICES-2018-81), NASA Ames; NASA NTRS citation 20200001093, https://ntrs.nasa.gov/citations/20200001093 . Reports Space Shuttle ~$54,500/kg and Falcon 9 ~$2,720/kg to LEO.
items.note: FIGURE DISAGREEMENT on the Shuttle number: the most-cited authoritative figure (Jones/NASA) is ~$54,500/kg, not the book's ~$30,000/kg. The book's Shuttle figure is roughly half the commonly cited per-kg cost; recommend the author either revise to ~$54,000/kg or footnote the lower number's basis (some analyses use marginal rather than fully-loaded cost). Falcon 9 ~$2,720/kg is squarely inside the book's $2,000-5,000/kg band (sourced clean). The ~$200-1,000/kg future figure is correctly hedged as 'plausible-but-unvalidated' in the chapter; leave as actor-projected target.

items.claim: Operational-status claim (line 241): 'Vulcan Centaur and New Glenn now operational.'
items.source: Vulcan Centaur: first flight 2024-01-08; certified for NSSL and first national-security launch (USSF-106) 2025-08-12 (Wikipedia 'Vulcan Centaur'; SpaceNews; Air & Space Forces Magazine). New Glenn: first orbital flight 2025-01-16; first-stage landing on second flight 2025-11-13 (Wikipedia 'New Glenn').
items.note: DATE-LOCK REQUIRED for 2026-06-04 publication. Both vehicles reached orbit (so 'operational' was true through 2025), BUT as of early 2026 BOTH have hit pauses: Vulcan launches were PAUSED in Feb 2026 pending a second solid-rocket-booster anomaly investigation, and New Glenn has one configuration inactive after a 2026 explosion that damaged the launch pad. The flat 'now operational' assertion is at risk of reading stale/wrong at publication. Recommend author re-verify both programs' status immediately before submission and consider hedging to 'have reached orbit' or dating the claim.

items.claim: Mars-surface delivery cost ~$2.5M/kg (line 243): Curiosity, Perseverance, and InSight each delivered ~1 kg of operational hardware to the Martian surface for ~$2.5M apiece.
items.source: Mission life-cycle costs: Curiosity ~$2.5B (inflation-adjusted ~$3.2B in 2020 dollars) and Perseverance ~$2.7B (~$2.9B inflation-adjusted) per Wikipedia 'Curiosity (rover)' and 'Perseverance (rover)' / The Planetary Society 'The Cost of Perseverance, in Context' (https://www.planetary.org/articles/cost-of-perseverance-in-context). Rover masses: Curiosity ~899 kg, Perseverance ~1,025 kg.
items.note: This is a CONSTRUCTED RATIO, not a directly published figure. $2.5B / ~1,000 kg landed rover mass = ~$2.5M/kg, which is internally consistent IF 'operational hardware' is read as the full rover (~1 tonne). The arithmetic is defensible. Caveat the author should weigh: if 'about a kilogram' is meant literally (~1 kg of payload), the derivation breaks; the figure only works as $/kg-of-landed-rover. Recommend the chapter footnote the derivation (mission cost / rover mass) so the constructed ratio is transparent rather than appearing to be a published per-kg quote.

items.claim: Asteroid-mining cost targets (line 243): Keck Institute Asteroid Retrieval Mission Study (2012) and Planetary Resources / Deep Space Industries projections targeting costs '5 to 7 orders of magnitude below current science-mission economics.'
items.source: John Brophy, Fred Culick, Louis Friedman et al., 'Asteroid Retrieval Feasibility Study,' Keck Institute for Space Studies (KISS), 2 April 2012, https://www.kiss.caltech.edu/final_reports/Asteroid_final_report.pdf . Targeted robotic retrieval of a ~7 m, ~500,000 kg near-Earth asteroid to high lunar orbit.
items.note: Keck 2012 study confirmed and citable directly. The company projections (Planetary Resources, Deep Space Industries) are correctly flagged by the chapter as actor-stated targets; both firms were acquired/defunct by 2018-2019, so frame as historical projections. The '5 to 7 orders of magnitude below science-mission economics' figure is a company/advocacy projection, not a Keck finding — keep attributed as an actor target, do not present as established.

items.claim: Lunar helium-3 stock (line 87): the Moon's regolith contains roughly a million tons.
items.source: L. J. Wittenberg, J. F. Santarius, G. L. Kulcinski, 'Lunar Source of 3He for Commercial Fusion Power,' Fusion Technology 10 (1986): 167-178 (Univ. of Wisconsin Fusion Technology Institute). Estimate of ~1 million tonnes of solar-wind-implanted 3He in near-surface lunar regolith.
items.note: Sourced. The ~1 million tonnes figure traces to Wittenberg/Santarius/Kulcinski 1986. The chapter is correct to treat it as 'commonly cited but contested' — the estimate depends on regolith depth/concentration assumptions and the recoverable fraction is far smaller. Recommend citing Wittenberg et al. 1986 and retaining the hedge.

items.claim: Stern vs. Nordhaus discount-rate framing (line 73): Stern Review near-zero pure rate of time preference vs. Nordhaus DICE higher market-calibrated rate; 'roughly 1.4 percent versus 4 percent' gap.
items.source: Stern Review on the Economics of Climate Change (HM Treasury, 2006): pure rate of time preference 0.1%, elasticity 1, growth 1.3% -> social discount rate ~1.4%. William Nordhaus, DICE model critique (e.g., 'A Review of the Stern Review on the Economics of Climate Change,' Journal of Economic Literature 45(3), 2007). See Wikipedia 'Stern Review' and RFF DP 07-37 'An Even Sterner Review.'
items.note: Stern's 1.4% is exactly sourced. Nordhaus's preferred rate: his critique cites a market/ethical range typically given as ~3-5% (often summarized as ~4.5% near-term in DICE runs). The book's '4 percent' sits inside that range and is a fair round-number stand-in, but it is not a single pinned Nordhaus constant — recommend phrasing as 'around 4-5 percent' or footnoting DICE's calibrated near-term rate rather than implying a fixed 4% value.

items.claim: Hartwick's rule (line 73): John Hartwick's 1977 rule that exhaustible-resource rents should be invested in reproducible capital to leave future generations no worse off.
items.source: John M. Hartwick, 'Intergenerational Equity and the Investing of Rents from Exhaustible Resources,' American Economic Review 67(5) (1977): 972-974.
items.note: Sourced and accurate, including the 1977 date, the AER venue, and the substantive claim. Cite as AER 67(5):972-974.

items.claim: Pyramids at Giza (line 213): built over ~70 years ~2550 BCE; workers paid in rations, medically cared for, properly buried (not chattel slaves); Lehner's Giza Plateau Mapping Project and Hawass's workers'-cemetery excavations as the revisionist consensus.
items.source: Mark Lehner, Giza Plateau Mapping Project / 'Lost City of the Pyramid Builders' (Ancient Egypt Research Associates); Zahi Hawass, Workers' Cemetery excavations at Giza. See NOVA/PBS 'Who Built the Pyramids?' and Harvard Magazine 'Who Built the Pyramids?' (2003); Mark Lehner, 'The Complete Pyramids' (1997).
items.note: The core revisionist claim is well-sourced: Lehner and Hawass document a fed, organized, paid (rationed) workforce with a workers' cemetery and on-site bakeries, contradicting the Herodotus slave narrative. One detail to verify against the prose: '~2550 BCE' and '~70 years' are reasonable for the Khufu-era Fourth Dynasty but the 70-year span conflates the building of multiple Giza pyramids (Khufu's Great Pyramid alone is conventionally ~20 years per Lehner). Recommend the author confirm whether the 70-year figure is meant for the whole Giza necropolis vs. a single pyramid.

items.claim: Qin China (line 215): Great Wall ~5,000 km; Sima Qian's Records of the Grand Historian reporting 300,000-500,000 concurrent conscripts on the Wall and ~700,000 on the mausoleum, with documented mass mortality; Terracotta Army discovered 1974; Qin dynasty collapsed within four years of the First Emperor's death.
items.source: Sima Qian, Records of the Grand Historian (Shiji), c. 94 BCE. Mausoleum: '~700,000 conscripts' (Shiji); see Wikipedia 'Mausoleum of Qin Shi Huang' and UNESCO WHC list 441. Terracotta Army discovered 1974 near Xi'an, Shaanxi (Wikipedia 'Terracotta Army'). Qin Shi Huang died 210 BCE; Qin fell 206 BCE.
items.note: Confirmed: ~700,000 mausoleum conscripts (Sima Qian), Terracotta Army discovered 1974, dynasty collapsed ~4 years after the First Emperor's 210 BCE death (fell 206 BCE). NOTE: the ~700,000 is the figure Sima Qian gives for the MAUSOLEUM, and the chapter correctly hedges Sima Qian's numbers as contested ('by his estimate'). The '300,000-500,000 on the Wall' figure is also from Sima Qian / early histories and is likewise contested by modern scholars — keep the hedge. The Great Wall '~5,000 km' refers to the Qin-era wall (the later Ming wall is ~8,850 km); fine as stated for the Qin context but verify the chapter is not conflating Qin and Ming wall lengths.

items.claim: Solar luminosity / Earth habitability horizon (line 197): rising main-sequence luminosity makes Earth uninhabitable for biological civilizations ~500 million to 1 billion years from now, well before the red-giant phase that engulfs Earth ~7.5 billion years out.
items.source: Red-giant/engulfment ~7.59 Gyr: K.-P. Schroder & R. C. Smith, 'Distant future of the Sun and Earth revisited,' Monthly Notices of the Royal Astronomical Society 386(1) (2008): 155-163, https://academic.oup.com/mnras/article/386/1/155/977315 . Biosphere-end ~0.5-1 Gyr from rising luminosity: standard result, e.g. K. Caldeira & J. F. Kasting (1992) and follow-on work.
items.note: Both numbers are correct but come from DIFFERENT results and the chapter should not attribute both to one source. The ~7.59 Gyr red-giant figure is Schroder & Smith 2008. The ~500 Myr-1 Gyr end-of-biosphere figure (from rising solar luminosity / runaway greenhouse, NOT the red-giant phase) is a separate standard finding (Caldeira & Kasting 1992; Wolf & Toon; etc.). Recommend citing Schroder & Smith 2008 for the engulfment horizon and a luminosity-evolution source for the ~1 Gyr biosphere limit; do not cite Schroder & Smith for the 500 Myr-1 Gyr number.

items.claim: Existential-risk lineage (line 141): Bostrom 2002 (existential-risk concept), Bostrom 2014 Superintelligence, MacAskill 2022 What We Owe the Future, Ord 2020 The Precipice, and the FHI / CSER literatures.
items.source: Nick Bostrom, 'Existential Risks: Analyzing Human Extinction Scenarios,' Journal of Evolution and Technology 9 (2002). Bostrom, Superintelligence: Paths, Dangers, Strategies (OUP, 2014). Toby Ord, The Precipice: Existential Risk and the Future of Humanity (2020). William MacAskill, What We Owe the Future (2022).
items.note: All four bibliographic anchors confirmed with correct dates: Bostrom 2002 (JET vol. 9), Bostrom 2014 (OUP), Ord 2020, MacAskill 2022. Future of Humanity Institute (Oxford) and Centre for the Study of Existential Risk (Cambridge) are correctly named. Date-lock minor note: FHI was closed in April 2024 — if the chapter implies FHI is extant, consider past-tense framing.

items.claim: Kimberley Process empirical claim (line 221): the 'conflict-free' diamond ethical premium 'stays with the retailer and the certification infrastructure; it does not reach the miners.'
items.note: No authoritative source pins the specific revenue-incidence mechanism (premium captured by retailer/certification vs. reaching miners). Supportive but non-dispositive context found: Global Witness reports artisanal diggers 'do not benefit' from downstream profit and ~1 million African alluvial miners earn <$1-2/day (Global Witness; Diamond Development Initiative); the KP is widely criticized for a narrow 'conflict' definition that ignores wages/conditions. To source the precise claim, look for: a peer-reviewed value-chain/incidence study of ethical-diamond premiums, a Global Witness or Human Rights Watch report quantifying premium pass-through, or academic supply-chain economics (e.g., journals on extractive-industry value capture). Recommend the author either soften to the defensible weaker claim ('the premium is not documented to reach miners; artisanal miners earn under $2/day') or supply a specific incidence study. Same caution applies to the downstream fair-trade-coffee, carbon-offset, and ESG-screen empirical claims if retained — each needs its own source.

items.claim: Illustrative-precedent attributions (line 109): 'Ostrom's eight design principles, Sen's ten capabilities, and Rawls's primary goods.'
items.source: Elinor Ostrom, Governing the Commons (Cambridge UP, 1990) — eight design principles. Amartya Sen, capability approach (Commodities and Capabilities, 1985; The Idea of Justice, 2009). John Rawls, A Theory of Justice (Harvard UP, 1971) — primary goods.
items.note: Ostrom's EIGHT design principles (1990) and Rawls's primary goods (1971) are exactly correct. CAUTION on 'Sen's ten capabilities': Sen deliberately declined to specify a fixed canonical list of capabilities — the well-known TEN-item 'central capabilities' list is Martha NUSSBAUM's, not Sen's. The book attributes a numbered ten-capability list to Sen, which is a misattribution. Recommend either (a) attribute the ten-item list to Nussbaum (Women and Human Development, 2000; Creating Capabilities, 2011), or (b) drop the number and refer to 'Sen's capability approach' without the count.

items.claim: Abundance-masking historical exemplars (lines 121/123): ocean fisheries before collapse; Ogallala aquifer abundance in the 1940s when central-pivot irrigation began drawing it down; atmosphere absorbing combusted carbon ~two centuries without observable saturation.
items.source: Ogallala/High Plains: large-scale groundwater irrigation expanded post-WWII; center-pivot irrigation patented by Frank Zybach in 1952 and scaled through the 1950s-60s (USGS High Plains Aquifer studies; Wikipedia 'Ogallala Aquifer'). Atmospheric CO2: industrial-era emissions since ~1750-1850 (IPCC AR6 WG1); Keeling curve from 1958.
items.note: FIGURE/DATE NOTE on Ogallala: large-scale well irrigation of the High Plains did begin ramping in the 1940s, but CENTER-PIVOT irrigation specifically was patented in 1952 and scaled in the 1950s-60s — so 'the 1940s when central-pivot irrigation began' is slightly early for center-pivot per se. Recommend 'the mid-20th century, as large-scale and then center-pivot irrigation began drawing it down,' or pin center-pivot to the 1950s. The '~two centuries' of atmospheric carbon absorption is fine if dated from ~1800 (early industrialization) to ~2000; consistent with IPCC framing. Ocean-fisheries-before-collapse is a sound general claim (e.g., cod collapse) but is illustrative, not a single-figure assertion.

items.claim: Mars-exploration mission roster (line 19): 'Mars 3, Viking I and II, Pathfinder, Spirit and Opportunity, Phoenix, Curiosity, InSight, Perseverance, with sample return and crewed proposals discussed for years' — supporting 'half a century' of active attempts and 'extensively measured' Martian-regolith parameter ranges.
items.source: The Planetary Society, 'Every mission to Mars ever' (https://www.planetary.org/space-missions/every-mars-mission); NASA, '25 Years of Continuous Robotic Mars Exploration.' Mars 3 (USSR) achieved the first soft landing 1971; Viking 1 & 2 (1976); Pathfinder/Sojourner (1997); Spirit & Opportunity (2004); Phoenix (2008); Curiosity (2012); InSight (2018); Perseverance (2021).
items.note: Roster is accurate. 'Half a century' is correct measured from Mars 3 (1971) to ~2021+. One framing note: Mars 3 is Soviet and was the first soft landing (1971) but transmitted for only ~20 seconds; the rest of the listed surface missions are US/NASA. The 'extensively measured Martian-regolith parameter ranges' claim is reasonable given the in-situ chemistry/soil data from Viking, Phoenix, the MER rovers, Curiosity, and Perseverance, but it is a synthesized characterization — if the chapter cites specific parameter ranges (temperature, perchlorate content, etc.) those specific numbers should each carry a NASA/mission-instrument source. As a general 'extensively measured' statement it is well supported.

items.claim: Direct health cost 'between two and four dollars per ton at the national level' (line 38), including Medicare/Medicaid shift, uncompensated care, bankrupt-employer private bills; only the $1-1.50 Black Lung subcomponent is program-anchored.
items.source: Aggregate anchorable to: Epstein PR, Buonocore J, Eckerle K, et al. 'Full cost accounting for the life cycle of coal.' Annals of the New York Academy of Sciences 1219:73-98 (2011). https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/21332493/
items.note: Epstein 2011 supplies the authoritative AGGREGATE external-health cost ($345B-$500B+/yr U.S.; ~$74.6B/yr Appalachian public-health burden) but does NOT publish a clean $/short-ton direct-health split. The all-in $2-4/ton is an author-derived per-ton allocation from the aggregate — keep flagged as internally-estimated-anchored-to-Epstein for the pre-pub queue. Only the $1-1.50 Black Lung subcomponent is independently program-anchored (Ch2).

items.claim: Excess cardiovascular mortality, respiratory illness in mining communities, cancer rates near processing facilities, and >10-year life-expectancy divergence (line 36).
items.source: Epstein et al. 2011, Annals NYAS 1219:73-98 (excess-mortality 1,736-2,889 deaths; cardiovascular/respiratory burden). Life-expectancy divergence: corroborated by Hendryx M. health-of-Appalachian-coal-communities series, e.g. Hendryx & Ahern, 'Mortality in Appalachian Coal Mining Regions,' Public Health Reports 124(4):541-550 (2009).
items.note: Mortality/morbidity elevation in MTR/coal counties is well-documented (Epstein 2011; Hendryx 2007-2014). The specific '>10-year life-expectancy divergence' magnitude was not pinned to a single source in this pass — author should attach the exact life-expectancy figure to a named Hendryx paper or a County Health Rankings / IHME locator. Body of the claim is sourced; the precise 10-year number needs a locator.

items.claim: Reclamation bond shortfall: 'available bonds total $3.8 billion against cleanup costs estimated at $7.5 to 9.8 billion; gap $4-6 billion' (line 48).
items.source: Appalachian Voices, 'No time to waste: averting another coal mine cleanup crisis' (Dec 15, 2023), citing its 2021 seven-Eastern-states analysis: $7.5-9.8B liabilities vs $3.8B available bonding. https://appvoices.org/2023/12/15/averting-another-coal-mine-cleanup-crisis/ . Federal context: GAO-18-305, 'Coal Mine Reclamation' (2018).
items.note: Book's exact figures ($3.8B bonds; $7.5-9.8B costs) MATCH the Appalachian Voices 2021 analysis (modern/active-mine reclamation in 7 Eastern states). The deep-pass §9 conflict with the ledger's '$4.5B (2021)' anchor is resolved: the $3.8B is the bonding-available figure, the $4.5B is a different anchor — author must DATE-LOCK to '(2021, Appalachian Voices)' and reconcile which figure the ledger cell holds. Distinct from the separate ~$11.5B historic AML (pre-1977) unfunded estimate (GAO-18-305) — do not conflate.

items.claim: Hendryx 60,000-cancer-case figure (line 50) needs the specific paper + year.

Cite Hendryx et al. 2012 (J Community Health) directly. Note it is a self-reported-survey extrapolation, not a registry count — flag the methodological basis.

items.claim: 'Hundreds of thousands of acres across Appalachia awaiting cleanup. Close to a million nationwide.' (line 48) abandoned-mine-land acreage.
items.source: OSMRE Abandoned Mine Land Inventory (e-AMLIS); Appalachian Citizens' Law Center / Alliance for Appalachia 2015 analysis (233,000 acres pre-1977 central-Appalachian AML); OSMRE program data (~800,000 acres reclaimed nationally to date).

Recommend author cite OSMRE e-AMLIS for the nationwide figure rather than leaving unsourced. Sourced with caveat that post-1977 sites are uninventoried.

items.claim: Atmospheric mercury from coal combustion showing up in North Atlantic fish (line 50).
items.source: Lee CS, Lutcavage ME, Chandler E, Madigan DJ, Cerrato RM, Fisher NS. 'Declining Mercury Concentrations in Bluefin Tuna Reflect Reduced Emissions to the North Atlantic Ocean.' Environmental Science & Technology 50(23):12825-12830 (2016).
items.note: Directly links coal-combustion atmospheric mercury emissions to methylmercury in North Atlantic bluefin tuna. Mechanism (coal emission -> atmospheric transport -> ocean deposition -> methylation -> bioaccumulation in apex predators) is well-established. Claim is sourced.

items.claim: Environmental degradation cost allocated at '$1-3 per ton' (line 52).
items.note: No external source publishes a $1-3/short-ton environmental-degradation allocation; this is an author-derived per-ton figure. The underlying damages (acid mine drainage, mercury, reclamation shortfall) ARE sourceable (above), but the per-ton allocation methodology is internal. Flag as internally-estimated for pre-pub queue. Author could anchor the aggregate to Epstein 2011 and disclose the per-ton division as the book's own apportionment.

items.claim: McDowell County demographics: peak ~100,000 in 1950; <20,000 by 2020; 2022 median household income $28,000, ~38% poverty, 16th-poorest; national median over $67,000 (line 58).

items.note: Figures CONFIRMED and close: book $28,000 vs $28,235; ~38% vs 37.6%; <20,000 vs 19,111 (2020); national $67,000+ vs $67,521. TIME-SENSITIVE (today 2026-06-04): date-lock all to '(2022 ACS / 2020 Census)' explicitly; 2023+ ACS releases will shift these. 1950 peak ~100,000 and 16th-poorest ranking are widely reported but author should attach the 1950 Census decennial cite and the source of the '16th-poorest' ranking (which year's ranking?).

items.claim: U.S. Steel mine closure 'in 1986' dropped county personal income by two-thirds in a single year (line 58); deep-pass flags the hard year 1986 as contested/unsourced.
items.source: West Virginia Encyclopedia (e-WV), 'Gary' entry (wvencyclopedia.org/entries/2025); corroborated by World Socialist Web Site, 'Economic transformation of Welch, West Virginia' (2010). U.S. Steel's Gary operations closed 1986.

These are secondary, not a primary BEA/Census series. RECOMMENDATION: keep the dated '1986' (it is supportable) but attach the e-WV citation; for the 'two-thirds in a single year' magnitude, ideally locate the BEA county personal-income series (CAINC1) to verify rather than rely on secondary characterization. Do NOT soften to 'across the 1980s' — the hard year is sourceable.

items.claim: 2015 drug-induced death rate '141 per 100,000' (highest of any U.S. county) vs ~16 national (line 58).
items.source: CDC WONDER / NCHS county drug-overdose mortality data, 2015: McDowell County WV 141.2 per 100,000 (highest of any U.S. county). National drug-overdose rate 2015 ~16.3 per 100,000 (CDC NCHS).

Lock the year in the prose.

items.claim: Community-disruption cost: 'in the range of five to fifteen dollars per ton' (line 62); deep-pass §9 calls this the least-sourced component figure and second-largest non-carbon head ($5).

Do not present as externally sourced.

items.claim: 'Fossil fuel industry annual lobbying spend runs into the hundreds of millions of dollars' (line 124).
items.source: OpenSecrets, Oil & Gas Lobbying Profile (id=E01): oil & gas ~$124.4M federal lobbying (2022), ~$150M (2024); broader energy/natural-resources sector ~$435M (2024). https://www.opensecrets.org/federal-lobbying/industries/summary?id=E01

items.claim: John L. Lewis / UMW 'declined to press' black-lung compensation 'reportedly on the grounds that raising it would interfere with mechanization' (line 120).

Most likely authoritative source: Alan Derickson, 'Black Lung: Anatomy of a Public Health Disaster' (Cornell UP, 1998), or Barbara Ellen Smith, 'Digging Our Own Graves: Coal Miners and the Struggle over Black Lung Disease' (1987). Author should source to Derickson/Smith before retaining; if not sourceable, soften or cut the motivation attribution per the no-invented-claims discipline.

items.claim: Black lung 'identifiable as an occupational disease by the early twentieth century'; '1969 recognition only after a miners' strike and the West Virginia Black Lung Association forced the legislative issue' (line 120).
items.source: MSHA, 'Federal Coal Mine Health and Safety Act of 1969' (msha.gov); the 1968 Farmington WV disaster (78 deaths) -> 1969 WV wildcat strikes + WV Black Lung Association -> Federal Coal Mine Health and Safety Act 1969 (enacted/signed Dec 1969, effective 1970). Scholarly: Derickson, 'Black Lung' (1998).
items.note: CONFIRMED. The strike-driven path to the 1969 Act and the WV Black Lung Association's role are well-documented. The 'identifiable by early 20th century' claim is also standard medical-history (British 'miner's asthma'/anthracosis literature). Sourced; recommend a Derickson cite for the historical lineage and MSHA for the Act.

items.claim: Obama '$42' and Biden '$51' interim SCC values (line 122).
items.source: Biden $51: Interagency Working Group, 'Technical Support Document: Social Cost of Carbon...' (Feb 2021 interim estimates, $51/ton 2020, 3% discount). Obama: IWG 2013/2016 TSD (~$42/ton 2020, updated). https://www.whitehouse.gov/wp-content/uploads/2021/02/TechnicalSupportDocument_SocialCostofCarbonMethaneNitrousOxide.pdf
items.note: Biden $51 CONFIRMED (Feb 2021 IWG interim TSD). Obama figure: commonly cited as ~$42-43/ton (2020 value, 3% discount); CONFIRMED in ballpark. CORRECTION FLAG: book attributes the Obama value to 'Executive Order 12866 implementation.' EO 12866 (1993) is the cost-benefit-analysis EO; the SCC value itself came from the IWG Technical Support Documents (2010/2013/2016), used IN regulatory analyses governed by EO 12866. Author should cite the IWG TSD as the source of the $42, not EO 12866 as the source. Minor framing correction.

items.claim: Temporal displacement: '1970s oil shocks produced a fifty-percent improvement in U.S. energy intensity over the subsequent two decades' (line 138).

items.note: Directionally CONFIRMED and conservative. EIA shows ~40% reduction in oil/gas energy intensity 1973-1985 and ~2%/yr ongoing decline; a ~50% improvement over two decades (to early 1990s) is consistent. Recommend citing EIA's energy-intensity series directly and specifying the metric (total energy/GDP vs oil-and-gas/GDP) and endpoints to pin the exact 50%.

items.claim: Cross-case IPGs (line 178): Deepwater Horizon IPG 15-17; Macondo projected 40-60M barrels -> 17-26M tons CO2 -> $3-5B carbon tail; Libby/W.R. Grace IPG 40-80; Baotou rare-earth; Exxon Valdez 11M gallons.

If '40-60M barrels' instead refers to total RESERVOIR estimate (not spilled volume), the prose must say so — as written it reads as the spill, which is contradicted by the court-found 3.19M bbl. AUTHOR MUST verify what the 40-60M figure denotes and confirm the 'see Ch5' grounding. The IPG numbers (15-17, 40-80) are book-internal framework metrics — verify against Ch5, not externally researchable.

items.claim: Tar sands First Nations cancer/contamination claims (line 182): elevated downstream cancer rates, contaminated traditional-food sources, Treaty 8 court engagement; Athabasca Chipewyan, Mikisew Cree, Fort McKay First Nations.

Book should attribute the elevated rates to the studies and treat the oil-sands CAUSATION as contested/alleged, not settled. Treaty 8 (1899) and the three named First Nations are accurate. Sourced for the rate elevation; flag the causation framing.

items.claim: DOJ RealPage timeline 'proposed settlement in late 2025' (line 192) — forward-dated, needs re-verification as past-tense fact.

Note: the settlement includes no financial penalty/admission and requires court approval — author should phrase as 'proposed settlement' (which the prose does) and may note approval was still pending. Update the tense to past.

items.claim: Across-market rise in cost-burdened renter share (line 190), and the causal claim that 'asset-class consolidation is producing it, not supply restriction' (line 192).

items.note: The DESCRIPTIVE claim (cost-burden share rose across markets) is sourced to JCHS 2024. The CAUSAL claim — that asset-class consolidation rather than supply restriction is producing it — is an interpretive argument NOT supported by JCHS (which does not adjudicate that causal question) and is contested by the Glaeser/Gyourko/Schuetz supply-side literature the chapter itself steelmans. Flag: present the causal claim as the book's argument, not as a sourced finding.

items.claim: 2008 crisis figures (line 196): $11T household wealth destroyed; ~10M foreclosure filings over five years; ~5M completed foreclosures; $700B TARP authorization plus trillions in liquidity/guarantees.

Household wealth: Federal Reserve Financial Accounts (~$11-13T net-worth decline 2007-2009). Foreclosure counts (~10M filings / ~5M completed) are RealtyTrac/CoreLogic figures — author should attach the specific RealtyTrac year-range citation; I did not independently pin the exact completed-foreclosure count this pass. Largely sourced; attach Fed Z.1 + RealtyTrac locators.

items.claim: Civilizational extrapolation (lines 218-220): global fossil-fuel revenue ~$4-5T/yr; full-cost factor 2-3x; resulting $8-10T fossil severed cost and $10-15T all-sector annual figure.

Author must confirm this stays explicitly framed as a gestured order-of-magnitude estimate, not a sourced figure. Pre-pub queue.

items.claim: Leaded gasoline: banned, refineries reformulated, industry did not collapse, children's cognitive outcomes improved (line 224) — historical precedent.

items.note: CONFIRMED on all four sub-claims: regulated/banned (EPA, on-road ban 1986); refineries reformulated and the petroleum industry did not collapse (historical fact); children's blood-lead and cognitive outcomes improved (CDC NHANES; lead-IQ literature). Strong, well-sourced historical precedent. Cite EPA phaseout record + CDC NHANES blood-lead trend.

items.claim: Solar electricity 'now cheaper than coal in most of the developing world' (line 210).

items.note: CONFIRMED. IRENA 2024 and Lazard 2024 both support solar being cheaper than coal across most markets including key developing economies (China, India). TIME-SENSITIVE/DATE-LOCK: cite to '(IRENA 2024 / Lazard 2024)'; LCOE figures move annually. Minor caveat IRENA flags: high capital/financing costs in some Global South markets (esp. parts of Africa) inflate LCOE there — 'most of the developing world' is accurate but not universal; consider 'much of' for precision.

items.claim: Aggregate '$10–15 trillion per year of severed costs' (line 110) — asserted bare in Ch9.

Closest external anchors: TEEB/Trucost 'Natural Capital at Risk: The Top 100 Externalities of Business' (2013, commissioned by UNEP) puts the top-100 environmental externalities at ~$4.7T/yr and total unpriced natural capital across primary production/processing at ~$7.3T/yr (~13% of 2009 GDP). The book's figure is described as deriving from Ch8/Appendix — treat as an INTERNAL-DERIVATION item: add an explicit in-text pointer to the Ch8/Appendix derivation rather than an external cite, OR reconcile the $10–15T figure against TEEB if an external anchor is wanted. Also: deep-pass §8 flags HIGH figure-drift in the related per-ton McDowell figures (opening '$500–600' / line-116 '$550' overshoot the ratified $510); confirm internal consistency.

items.claim: Mian & Sufi: bottom-40% welfare benefit by a factor of 3–4 relative to top-quintile households from debt restructuring vs bank rescue (line 122).
items.source: Atif Mian & Amir Sufi, House of Debt (Univ. of Chicago Press, 2014).

Web search surfaced only the book's general thesis (debt contracts force risk onto those least able to bear it; equity-based 'shared-responsibility mortgages' as the fix), not the quantified 3–4x distributional figure.

items.claim: 2008-crisis macro figures: ~$11T US household wealth destroyed; ~10 million foreclosures over five years; ~$700B TARP direct authorization (line 122).
items.source: Financial Crisis Inquiry Commission, The Financial Crisis Inquiry Report (US GPO, 2011); Federal Reserve Flow of Funds / Financial Accounts (Z.1); Emergency Economic Stabilization Act of 2008 (TARP, P.L. 110-343).
items.note: ~$11T household wealth: SOURCED — household net worth fell from ~$61.4T to ~$50.4T by Q1 2009 (Fed Z.1), matches. TARP $700B original authorization: SOURCED (EESA 2008; later capped at $475B by Dodd-Frank; ~$443.5B disbursed) — 'direct authorization' framing is correct. FORECLOSURES DISAGREE: the standard figures are ~4 million families losing homes 2008–2010 and ~6 million homes lost to foreclosure over the broader crisis; the book's '~10 million foreclosures over five years' is on the HIGH end — defensible only if counting all foreclosure FILINGS/starts (not completed losses). Flag: clarify 'foreclosure filings' vs 'homes lost,' or revise toward 4–6M.

items.claim: Sweden 1931–1938 historical chain: 1931 gold-standard exit, mortgage write-downs, public-works employment, 1938 Saltsjöbaden Agreement durability through WWII/1973/1992/present, 1992 banking-crisis equity-capture recovery (line 126).
items.source: Riksbank historical record (Sweden left the gold standard 28 Sept 1931); Saltsjöbaden Agreement, signed by LO and SAF 20 December 1938.
items.note: PARTIALLY SOURCED. 1931 gold-standard exit: SOURCED (28 Sept 1931). 1938 Saltsjöbaden Agreement (labor-employer wage-bargaining 'peace obligation'): SOURCED. CAUTION on durability: scholarship describes the 'Saltsjöbaden spirit' as characterising labour policy 'at least until the late 1960s' — the unqualified 'durability through WWII/1973/1992/present' overstates continuity (the centralized model frayed from the 1970s–80s). The 1992 Swedish banking-crisis equity-capture/recovery-with-countercyclical-fiscal claims were NOT confirmed in this pass and need a dedicated source (e.g., Englund's accounts of the 1990–94 Swedish banking crisis / Securum bad-bank). Recommend softening the durability claim and citing the 1992 crisis specifically.

items.claim: Baotou: 'seven square miles of radioactive slurry' tailings pond bordering villages (line 92).
items.source: Baogang (Baotou) tailings dam reporting; in-situ gamma-ray surveys of Baotou/Bayan Obo tailings (e.g., Journal of Environmental Radioactivity).
items.note: FIGURE DISAGREES. The Baotou (Baogang) tailings dam is widely reported at ~10 km² ≈ ~3.9 square miles — NOT seven square miles. The radioactivity/villages substance is solid (Th-232 ~34x local soil; village of Dalahai affected; leaching toward the Yellow River). Recommend revising 'seven square miles' down to ~4 square miles / ~10 square kilometres, or cite a specific source supporting a larger area.

items.claim: Niger Delta: fifty years of spillage rendered thousands of sq km of mangrove/farmland uninhabitable (line 92).

UNEP 2011 documents pollution from 'over 50 years of oil operations,' disastrous mangrove impacts (roots coated in bitumen-type substance ≥1cm), contaminated farmland/drinking water, and that heavily-impacted mangrove/swamp restoration will take up to 30 years. Note UNEP's assessment focused on Ogoniland specifically; 'thousands of square kilometres' across the wider Delta is consistent with the literature but the UNEP report is the safe anchor. Use UNEP (2011) as the cite.

items.claim: DRC cobalt: ~40,000 children have worked in artisanal mines; cobalt 'overwhelmingly extracted from the DRC until very recently' (line 92).
items.source: Amnesty International & Afrewatch, 'This is What We Die For: Human Rights Abuses in the DRC Power the Global Trade in Cobalt' (AFR 62/3183/2016); 40,000-children figure originates with UNICEF (2014).
items.note: SOURCED with attribution caveat: the 40,000-children figure is UNICEF's 2014 estimate of children in southern-DRC mines (many but not all mining cobalt), cited by Amnesty 2016 — attribute to UNICEF (2014) via Amnesty, not to Amnesty alone. Temporal hedge 'until very recently' is fair: DRC supplies ~70%+ of global cobalt; Indonesia has grown as a secondary source recently, so 'overwhelmingly... until very recently' is defensible. Keep the hedge.

items.claim: Alaska Permanent Fund: 25% of mineral royalty captured since 1976; ~$80B corpus; dividend to every resident since 1982, most recently $1,300–$1,700 (line 118).
items.source: Alaska Permanent Fund Corporation (apfc.org) + Alaska Permanent Fund Dividend Division (pfd.alaska.gov); Alaska Constitution Art. IX §15 (1976).
items.note: SOURCED, with a figure to date-lock. 25% of mineral royalties since 1976 (constitutional): correct. First dividend 1982 ($1,000): correct. CORPUS: ~$80B was accurate ~2022–23 but the fund is now ~$89B (April 2026) — TIME-SENSITIVE, date-lock as 'roughly $80 billion (2023)' or update. DIVIDEND RANGE: the 2024 PFD was $1,702, which slightly EXCEEDS the book's '$1,300–$1,700' top; recent dividends have ranged ~$1,000–$3,284 (2022 incl. energy relief). Recommend a year-anchored phrasing, e.g., 'in the $1,300–$1,700 range in recent years (the 2024 dividend was $1,702).'

items.claim: Tax Foundation 2024: tax wedge ~30% US / ~42% Sweden; tax-to-GDP ~26% US / ~41% Sweden (line 198) — basis of the 'eleven-cent difference.'
items.source: Tax Foundation, 'A Comparison of the Tax Burden on Labor in the OECD, 2024' (tax wedge); Tax Foundation country data / OECD Revenue Statistics (tax-to-GDP).

Tax-to-GDP 2024: US 25.6%, Sweden 41.4% — matches '~26% / ~41%'. The 11-point wedge gap and ~15-point tax-to-GDP gap both hold. Cite the specific 2024 Tax Foundation labor-burden report for the wedge and the country/OECD figure for tax-to-GDP. Confirm 'eleven-cent' framing maps to the 11-point wedge difference (it does).

items.claim: Universal-coverage/outcomes comparison (Sweden, Denmark, Norway, Germany, UK, Canada, Australia) and '~28 million uninsured' US figure (line 200).

Mirror Mirror 2024: US ranks LAST overall among 10 high-income countries; top performers Australia, Netherlands, UK — supports the favorable-comparison claim for the named countries. UNINSURED FIGURE DISAGREES SLIGHTLY: Census/Commonwealth Fund put 2023 uninsured at ~26 million (8%), not ~28 million; '~28 million' may reflect an older year or a different measure. Recommend revising to '~26 million (2023)' or anchoring the year for the 28M source. Cite OECD Health Statistics for the specific outcome metrics (life expectancy, infant/maternal mortality).

items.claim: Berliner & Kenworthy 2017: 'over 250,000 medical-crowdfunding campaigns annually'; '25–40% of stated need' (line 202).
items.source: Lauren S. Berliner & Nora Kenworthy, 'Producing a worthy illness: Personal crowdfunding amidst financial crisis,' Social Science & Medicine 187 (2017): 233–242; the 250,000/year figure is GoFundMe's own reporting (not Berliner & Kenworthy).
items.note: ATTRIBUTION MISMATCH. Berliner & Kenworthy (2017) analyzed a SMALL randomized sample (~200 GoFundMe campaigns), documenting that the large majority fail to reach their goal — it is NOT the source of '250,000 campaigns annually.' The 250,000/year (raising >$650M) figure is GoFundMe's self-reported statistic, widely cited (e.g., CBS News). Recommend splitting the cite: attribute the 250,000/year scale figure to GoFundMe reporting, and the success-rate/coverage-shortfall pattern to Berliner & Kenworthy (2017). Verify the exact '25–40% of stated need' figure against the paper; the study's headline is that ~90% of campaigns missed their goal.

items.claim: Ly & Soman 2020 and Kenworthy & Igra 2024 confirm crowdfunding scale + differential success rates (line 202).
items.note: Not located with confidence in this pass. items.source: John Rawls, A Theory of Justice, Harvard University Press, 1971 (rev. ed. 1999), §45 'Time Preference' (1971 ed. ~pp. 293-298). Rawls's actual verbatim formulation: parties in the original position have 'no pure time preference,' and 'there is no reason for the parties to give any weight to mere position in time' (1971 ed. ~p. 294). Following Sidgwick, Rawls rejects pure time discounting.
items.note: The quoted sentence does NOT appear to be Rawls's verbatim wording -- it is a paraphrase/composite of his §45 position. Either (i) replace with the verbatim Rawls sentence + exact page from a specific edition, or (ii) reframe as a paraphrase ('Rawls argues, in effect, that...') and drop the quotation marks. Verify against the physical text before submission; do not ship invented-verbatim quotation marks around a paraphrase. Confirm which edition (1971 vs 1999 revised) the page cite refers to, since pagination differs.

items.claim: §9.3 'solar at $0.03/kWh' and §16.2 'stranded asset risk' mechanism -- public-domain figures asserted without a source.
items.source: IRENA, Renewable Power Generation Costs in 2024 (published July 2025): global weighted-average utility-scale solar PV LCOE = $0.043/kWh in 2024; USA = $0.070/kWh; lowest national markets (China) ~$0.033/kWh. Lazard LCOE+ (2025): US utility-scale solar $38-78/MWh ($0.038-0.078/kWh).
items.note: DISAGREES (overstated-low) AND TIME-SENSITIVE. The book's '$0.03/kWh' is below both the IRENA 2024 global average ($0.043) and the US figure ($0.070); $0.03 is reached only in the very cheapest markets. Recommend revising to a sourced range -- e.g., 'utility-scale solar LCOE of roughly $0.04/kWh globally (IRENA 2024), as low as ~$0.03/kWh in the cheapest markets' -- and cite IRENA or Lazard. DATE-LOCK: LCOE figures update annually; as of 2026-06-04 the latest is IRENA's 2024 cost report (pub. mid-2025). Re-lock before submission against any 2025-cost report.

items.claim: Bibliography-completeness gaps: Rennert et al. 2022 (the SCC $190 source), NBIM Annual Report 2025, Norges Bank, Norwegian Petroleum Directorate, Statistics Norway, Daly ('Daly 1990s'), and Brian Barry have no §18 entries; inline 'Nordhaus (2007)' / 'Stern (2006)' years mismatch bibliography 'Nordhaus & Boyer 2000' / 'Stern 2007'.
items.source: Rennert et al.: K. Rennert, F. Errickson, B.C. Prest, et al., 'Comprehensive evidence implies a higher social cost of CO2,' Nature 610: 687-692 (2022), DOI 10.1038/s41586-022-05224-9 -- central SCC = $185/t-CO2 (2020 USD, 2% discount rate). NBIM: Government Pension Fund Global Annual Report 2025, Norges Bank Investment Management (nbim.no). Stern Review: N. Stern, The Economics of Climate Change: The Stern Review, Cambridge University Press, 2007 (commissioned 2006). Nordhaus: W. Nordhaus, A Question of Balance, Yale University Press, 2008 (or Nordhaus & Boyer, Warming the World, MIT Press, 2000). Daly: H.E. Daly, 'Sustainable Growth: An Impossibility Theorem,' in Daly & Townsend (eds.), Valuing the Earth, MIT Press, 1993 (orig. 1990).
items.note: CRITICAL ATTRIBUTION CORRECTION: the SCC figure of $190/ton is NOT Rennert 2022 -- Rennert et al. report $185/t-CO2 (2020 USD). The $190 value is the EPA's 2023 updated central estimate (EPA, Report on the Social Cost of Greenhouse Gases, Nov. 2023), which drew on Rennert/RFF work. If the book uses $190 it should cite EPA 2023, not Rennert 2022; if it cites Rennert it should say $185. Reconcile this everywhere $190 appears (incl. the §11.1 McDowell carbon term in item 1). For Nordhaus/Stern year mismatches: the inline 'Stern (2006)' refers to the commission year and 'Stern 2007' to publication -- standardize to one. 'Nordhaus (2007)' likely means the 2007 JEL review 'A Review of the Stern Review' (J. Economic Literature 45(3): 686-702) -- distinct from Nordhaus & Boyer 2000; clarify which is meant. Brian Barry: likely B. Barry, 'Sustainability and Intergenerational Justice,' Theoria 45(89): 43-64 (1997) or Theories of Justice (1989) -- author must confirm which Barry work is intended before a §18 entry can be pinned (author-confirmation needed on this sub-item).
